Regression models evaluated associations between engagement states and later receptive and expressive language.
Time spent in supported joint engagement was significantly associated with later receptive language only. Higher-order supported joint engagement had a significant positive association with later receptive language, whereas lower-order supported joint engagement did not. This association was stronger for Sibs-autism. 
These results provide insights into how caregiver–child engagement relates to later language in Sibs-autism and Sibs-NA. Further work is necessary to understand how to best support caregivers to optimize language learning environments for toddlers.

articles.title: Modeling Item Difficulty in Large-Scale Game-Based Cognitive Ability Assessment: An Illustration Using Wordle
articles.authors: Priscilla Achaa-Amankwaa; Jonas Walter; Valentin Hübner; Philipp Hübner; Ulrich Schroeders
articles.abstract: Building on the long and successful tradition of incorporating games into cognitive ability assessment, we investigated the popular word puzzle game Wordle, which integrates verbal abilities (lexical knowledge, orthographic processing), strategic word retrieval, and reasoning. Specifically, we examined the extent to which item difficulty in the German adaptation, GridWords, can be predicted using linguistic features, using a large online database that comprised 872 items collected from 76,095 players over two years. Item difficulty was operationalized via the number of attempts needed to solve an item, with scores adjusted for possible within-person practice effects across repeated gameplay. Moving beyond previous approaches that were limited to narrow sets of predictors, we integrated a broad set of features from psycholinguistic research on word recognition, such as word and letter frequencies, orthographic neighborhood size, and letter repetitions. Using regularized machine-learning methods in a nested cross-validation approach, we found out-of-sample predictive performances of R² = 52% (elastic net regression) and R² = 58% (gradient boosting machines) in item difficulty prediction. The cumulative consonant and unigram position frequencies, number of letter repetitions, and number of orthographic neighbors of the target words ranked among the most important features. We discuss feature importances as indicators of the underlying cognitive processes during gameplay, particularly with regard to the relevance of implicit knowledge of lexical regularities and the use of deliberate strategies. We also consider the strengths and limitations of using online games for cognitive ability assessment.

articles.title: Memories of Sexual Abuse in the Distant Past: Subtypes and Corroboration in a Legal Context
articles.authors: Mirjam ter Beek; Henry Otgaar; Linsey Raymaekers; Corine de Ruiter; Paul Riesthuis; Tamara L. F. De Beuf; Anne Hoving; Meike Hogenkamp; Bruno Verschuere
articles.abstract: Objective: Assessing the validity of testimony is critical in sexual abuse cases, particularly in case of recovered memories, whose authenticity has been considered controversial. The current study examined whether different types of memories (i.e., continuous, spontaneously recovered, recovered following an active search) could be reliably distinguished and whether they differed in the extent to which they are supported by evidence. Hypotheses: We predicted that three types of memories could be reliably differentiated: Continuous memories, spontaneously recovered memories, and recovered memories following an active search. We further predicted that continuous memories and spontaneously recovered memories would be more frequently corroborated by evidence than recovered memories following an active search. Method: We analyzed 140 police files on complex cases of sexual abuse in the distant past in which the prosecutor requested advice from an expert committee of behavioral experts. One pair of coders identified memory type based on continuity and retrieval, and another set of coders scored the degree of supporting evidence. Results: Memory types could be differentiated reliably. Statements from (alleged) victims in the continuous memory group were corroborated more often than those in the recovered memory-following-search group, which were rarely supported by strong evidence. The data were inconclusive regarding whether recovered memories following an active search and spontaneously recovered memories differ in the extent to which they were corroborated by evidence. Exploratory analyses showed that spontaneously recovered memories were more often related to single incidents, whereas recovered memories following an active search frequently involved sexual abuse over an extended period, including reports of multiple perpetrators. Conclusions: Reports of sexual abuse in the distant past can be reliably categorized into three types that help shed light on the likelihood of finding corroborating evidence in a criminal context. Recovered memories should not be treated as a homogeneous phenomenon because their phenomenology varies.

articles.title: Loneliness in the Human Brain: Structural and Functional MRI Correlates and Research Priorities
articles.authors: Christian K. Tamnes; Livia Tomova
articles.abstract: Loneliness, the subjective experience of a perceived discrepancy between desired and actual social connection, is robustly associated with poor mental and physical health across the lifespan. Although not uniformly increasing in prevalence, loneliness is rising among adolescents and young adults, making it an important public health concern. Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) offers a means to examine the neural pathways through which loneliness emerges and affects health. A growing body of structural and functional MRI research implicates differences in regions and networks central to social cognition, internally directed thought, episodic memory, and executive control, including most consistently the default mode network, medial temporal structures, and prefrontal cortex. Here, we review this literature, critically assess its limitations, and outline priorities for future research, including the need for refined measurement, longitudinal developmental and experimental designs, and integration with genetic and contextual perspectives.

articles.title: How to tell whether AI is behaviourally human-like
articles.authors: Jamal Amani Rad; Stephane Hess; Thomas Hancock
articles.abstract: AI systems are increasingly described as behaving like humans. But passing as human in conversation, sharing answers or achieving similar benchmark scores is not enough to justify that specific label. Drawing on behavioural modelling, we propose five practical tests for deciding when claims of behavioural human-likeness have genuinely been earned.

articles.title: Prosody and Voice Quality in the Connected Speech of Individuals with Latent Aphasia
articles.authors: Cong Zhang; Tong Li; Gayle Dede; Chris Salis
articles.abstract:
Purpose: We explored the prosody and voice quality of people with latent aphasia, a mild form of aphasia, against a neurotypical control group. In contrast to people with moderate to severe aphasia, people with latent aphasia can have similar speech production compared to neurotypical controls. 

Method: We analysed three prosodic measures (fundamental frequency [f0], intensity, and duration) and three voice quality measures (local jitter, local shimmer, and harmonic-to-noise ratio) of utterance-initial and utterance-final words from ten speakers with latent aphasia and ten matched controls. Three relevant linguistic features (syllable number, part of speech, and word type [content or function words]) were also considered. Linear mixed-effects models assessed the effects of word position and speaker group on prosodic features.

Results: Both groups showed parallel patterns in f0 and intensity: Utterance-final words were produced with higher f0 and lower intensity than utterance-initial words. Significant group-by-position interactions indicated that these measures were reduced in the latent aphasia group, suggesting subtle but systematic prosodic distinctions. Word duration further differentiated the two groups, with the latent group consistently showing longer duration than controls. Voice quality measures showed minimal group differences, consistent with the latent group being clinically classified as “normal.” Nonetheless, these measures provide baseline values for spontaneous speech, which diverge from cut-offs based on sustained vowels.

Conclusions: Prosodic features are sensitive indicators of latent aphasia, offering an explanation for why predictive models using prosody alone can successfully distinguish affected individuals from controls. Voice quality measures, while less informative for group separation, contribute novel data for this population. Together, the results underscore the importance of prosodic research for identifying subtle pathological differences and support future clinical and computational approaches to detecting clinical language disorders.

articles.title: Reducing Cognitive Rigidity in Psychotherapy: Effects of an Inspiration Tool on Creative Cognition in Adults With Psychiatric Disorders
articles.authors: Maya Wenzel; Marco Filippi; Anja Vandersmissen; Paula Kattermann; Anja Rauthmann; Charlotte P. Malycha; Jens C Pruessner; Rainer Krähenmann; Patrick Fissler Plischke
articles.abstract: Objective: Cognitive rigidity is a transdiagnostic process observed across various psychopathologies and commonly targeted in psychotherapy. This study investigated whether a random input technique (RIT)-based inspiration tool enhances creative cognition in adults with psychiatric disorders.  Method: A total of 100 adults with a lifetime psychiatric disorder were randomized to either an inspiration tool (Brain2Business™ small) combined with brainstorming rules (B2B + BS) or brainstorming rules alone (BS) in a participant-blinded randomized controlled trial. Both groups completed their respective condition during two face-to-face psychotherapeutic interventions, followed by home-based sessions over 5 days. General, subjective, and objective creative cognition—including fluency, flexibility, and originality—were assessed. Results: Compared with BS, the B2B + BS condition increased objective creative cognition (SMD = 0.37, p = .049), including fluency (SMD = 0.43, p = .024) and flexibility (SMD = 0.41, p = .034). No significant effects were found for other outcomes. Additional analyses showed that the effect on fluency of B2B + BS vs. BS increased over time during the two face-to-face sessions. Conclusion: Findings suggest that an RIT-based inspiration tool supports psychotherapeutic processes by reducing rigid thinking patterns, particularly at later stages of idea generation.

articles.title: At what sample size do correlations stabilize with unicorn and other improbable creatures?
articles.authors: Anthony Durand; Clément Guichet
articles.abstract: To ensure sufficient statistical power, researchers are encouraged to justify their sample size through power analysis (Lakens, 2022; Cohen, 1988). One complementary approach estimates the required sample size based on the stability of the anticipated effect δ (Schönbrodt & Perugini, 2013). However, this approach was originally developed assuming normal distributions, and no simulation studies have examined its robustness under non-normal data. We therefore conducted simulations following Schönbrodt and Perugini's (2013) procedure to estimate the point of stability across various non-normal distributions. Results indicated that small-to-moderate normality violations only slightly affected the point of stability, whereas strong violations produced substantial changes. Implications are discussed.

articles.title: Prediction in action: toward an empirical science of active inference
articles.authors: Jet Lageman; Johannes Jacobus Fahrenfort; Heleen A Slagter
articles.abstract: Active inference has emerged as an influential theoretical framework in cognitive neuroscience, offering a unifying account of perception, action, and cognition under the single principle of surprise minimization. Despite its growing prominence, the framework is often criticized for the difficulty of extracting qualitatively distinct, testable predictions and its limited empirical grounding. This review addresses these concerns by systematically outlining and evaluating key predictions of active inference across decision-making in discrete time and motor control in continuous time. For each domain, we offer a formal introduction of active inference and contrast it with leading alternative accounts. In the domain of decision-making, we identify predictions that agents behave more stochastically when they are uncertain about what to do; that they explore to resolve uncertainty about hidden states and model parameters; and that preferences can be learned through accumulated experience. In the domain of motor control, we identify predictions that sensory input is attenuated early and non-specifically to facilitate movement initiation; that motor output is context-sensitive and reflects predictions of proprioceptive input; and that the brain adaptively controls and predictively corrects movements depending on estimations of uncertainty. Overall, we find that existing empirical work is broadly consistent with several of these predictions, but further research is necessary to draw more definitive conclusions. We highlight areas where evidence is promising, while emphasizing the need for theory-driven experiments that can adjudicate between accounts. In this way, active inference can move beyond a formal mathematical framework toward an empirically grounded theory of brain function.

articles.title: People Underestimate the Acceptability of Canceling Plans with Others
articles.authors: Rajarshi Majumder; Esra Aslan; Jareef Martuza
articles.abstract: Consumers frequently cancel social consumption experiences with others, such as dining out or attending events. Do cancelers correctly infer the reactions of those who are canceled on? Correct estimation, overestimation, and underestimation are all plausible predictions from existing literature. Across 11 studies (N = 6,824) with American and French participants, this research shows that cancelers underestimate how acceptable others find being canceled on. This underestimation effect is robust across different plan types, whether alternatives to the original plan exist, the level of effort exerted by the person canceled on, and justifications for canceling. Findings also reveal that the effect is partly driven by the cancelers’ beliefs that others are less forgiving and more impacted by cancellations than they actually are. Consequently, cancelers also overestimate monetary amounts of gifts and the time commitment of favors that those canceled on expect as compensation. Interestingly, upfront compensatory action only partly attenuates the underestimation effect, suggesting the misperception is deep-rooted. Together, these findings extend consumer research on misperceptions about others, from social beliefs regarding plan cancellations to compensatory gift-giving.

articles.title: Optimized cardiorespiratory state at tone onset enhances sensory responses but not associative learning in healthy adults
articles.authors: Praghajieeth Raajhen Santhana Gopalan; Miriam S. Nokia
articles.abstract: Previous studies suggest that when a tone-conditioned stimulus (CS) predicting an airpuff towards the eye is systematically presented at diastole during expiration, both sensory responses to the CS and trace eyeblink conditioning (TEBC) are enhanced. However, not all participants learn and individual variation in the body-brain relationship remains overlooked. We studied whether timing the CS based on an individually optimised bodily state enhances both sensory responses and TEBC. We assumed so, as sensory response magnitude has been suggested to reflect attention and stimulus salience: A salient CS should facilitate TEBC. Forty-one young adults and 19 older adults completed a passive auditory task while electrocardiogram, respiration and electroencephalogram were recorded. The cardiorespiratory state associated with the largest N1–P2 response was identified individually. Optimal states varied across participants and did not differ between age groups. Participants then underwent TEBC either at their optimal state (Personalised) or at diastole during expiration (Control). As expected, the CS evoked a larger N1–P2 response in the Personalised than Control group. However, conditioned responding, learning rate or the proportion of good learners did not differ between groups. Older adults learned slower, made fewer conditioned responses, and were less aware of the tone-airpuff association than young adults. These findings suggest that successful TEBC is not so much dependent on handling of the CS but possibly on further processing within distributed neural networks. Future studies should examine whether timing external stimuli to specific bodily states more effectively influences behaviour in tasks that rely directly on rapid sensory processing.

articles.title: Bridging analysis and performance in elite Para swimming: Applied case studies in external focus, constraints-based learning, and practice variability
articles.authors: Danny Powell; Greg Wood; Neil Dagnall; Carl Payton
articles.abstract: The implementation of skill change in elite sport often fails not due to a lack of biomechanical insight, but because recommended changes do not transfer effectively into stable performance. British Para Swimming created a dedicated skill acquisition role to address this gap between analysis and applied coaching practice. This paper presents two applied case studies drawn from embedded practice within an elite Para swimming programme. The first illustrates how an external focus of attention facilitated rapid performance change, retention, and transfer in a swimmer with restricted working memory capacity. The second demonstrates how constraints-based learning and structured task variability supported the (re)acquisition of an automatic perceptual–motor skill (‘spotting the wall’) through implicit learning mechanisms. Both cases are interpreted through contemporary skill acquisition frameworks, including attentional focus theory, constraints-led approaches, the Challenge Point Framework, and the Periodisation of Skill Training (PoST) framework. Together, these cases highlight how theoretically informed adjustments to instructional design can produce meaningful and durable skill change in high-performance disability sport contexts.

articles.title: From Emotions to Achievement: Positioning Language Learning Curiosity in the Nomological Network of Individual Differences
articles.authors: Nazila Fattahi; Faramarz Ebn-Abbasi; Jean-Marc Dewaele; Imelda K. Brady; Elouise Botes
articles.abstract: The present study aimed to position foreign language curiosity within the nomological network of key emotional and behavioural variables in foreign language (FL) learning. Drawing on an international sample of 345 FL learners, we examined the relationships among curiosity and enjoyment, anxiety, and boredom, as well as their links to willingness to communicate (WTC), engagement, and academic achievement using a structural equation model. Results indicated that curiosity was strongly and positively associated with enjoyment, and negatively associated with anxiety and boredom, supporting its role as a positively valenced construct within the FL emotional system. Furthermore, curiosity emerged as a significant positive predictor of both WTC and engagement, alongside enjoyment, while anxiety negatively predicted WTC. In contrast, boredom did not significantly predict WTC, suggesting differential pathways between deactivating emotions and communicative behaviour. Both WTC and engagement were found to positively predict academic achievement. These findings highlight the central role of curiosity as an active motivational and emotional driver in language learning and underscore the need for future research to further embed curiosity in explanatory models of FL learning.

articles.title: Remembering What We Did: Confidence Tracks the Distinctiveness of Action Representations
articles.authors: Weronika Domagala; Kevin O'Neill; Maia Juliet Armstrong; Daniel Yon; Silvia Seghezzi
articles.abstract: We plan by considering multiple alternatives, yet only one action is ultimately performed. How memory later distinguishes executed actions from those that were merely considered, and what metacognitive confidence reveals about this distinction, remains unclear. Across two experiments, participants planned and executed routes through mazes before judging whether specific locations had belonged to the route they took or to an unexecuted 'path not taken'. Confidence was assessed either before or after route navigation. Confidence carried reliable trial-level information about the quality of route choice, indicating that it functioned as a genuine subjective signal about action-planning quality rather than as a global response tendency. Higher confidence was also associated with greater discriminability between executed and unexecuted routes in memory, indicating more distinct action representations, but showed no reliable relationship with the criterion used to endorse a memory. These effects were equivalent whether confidence was assessed before or after action execution. Together, these findings suggest that confidence indexes the precision of action representations during planning and that false memories for unexecuted alternatives arise from reduced representational distinctiveness rather than from changes in decision policy.

articles.title: Sensorimotor coupling modulates perceived time and agency across visual perspectives
articles.authors: Shu Imaizumi; Giuseppe Lai; Anil Seth; Keisuke Suzuki
articles.abstract: Voluntary actions are associated with sense of agency and distortions of perceived duration. This study examined how sensorimotor coupling and visual perspective modulate perceived duration and sense of agency during manual actions in four pre-registered experiments using virtual reality. Participants moved their hands while observing a virtual hand moving (a)synchronously, and evaluated movement duration and their sense of agency. We found that sensorimotor coupling provided by synchronous visual feedback is associated with longer perceived duration relative to action-related time compression, compared with delayed feedback or pre-recorded movements of another person (Experiments 1 and 2). Sensorimotor coupling also modulated sense of agency and may serve as a shared basis for perceived time and agency. Comparable modulations across first- and third-person perspectives (Experiments 1 and 2), anatomical configurations (Experiment 3), and attention on external entities (Experiment 4) suggest a shared predictive system for sensory consequences of self-generated actions and others’ reactions. This modulation was observed irrespective of anatomical configuration and when attention was directed toward objects unrelated to one’s own body, suggesting that the underlying mechanism is not confined to representations specific to the self, but may instead reflect domain-general predictive computations. These findings, together with the virtual reality technique developed, offer new insights into how humans experience passage of time and agency during voluntary action.

articles.title: Altered States, Enhanced Potential: Psychedelics and Physical Performance
articles.authors: Zane Qarni; Jeremie Richard
articles.abstract: Interest in psychedelics has expanded beyond clinical treatment contexts, yet little empirical work has examined how users describe psychedelic use in relation to sport and physical activity. This study conducted a qualitative content analysis of Reddit discussions involving psychedelics and physical performance. A Reddit search for “sports psychedelics” returned 253 threads. After screening, 39 threads and 290 comments met inclusion criteria. Analysis identified eight content categories: physical performance enhancement; flow state/automaticity; perceptual and cognitive enhancement; risks, limitations, and safety concerns; reduced fatigue/pain perception; microdosing as strategy; psychological and emotional benefits; and fairness, ethics, and governance. Psilocybin mushrooms and LSD were the most frequently mentioned substances. Across accounts, users most often described psychedelics as enhancing performance indirectly through altered attention, increased mind-body connection, flow-like absorption, and reduced pain or fatigue. Some users reported greater perceived strength, speed, endurance, coordination, or overall capability during activity, while others described effortless movement, reduced self-consciousness, sharper perception, and improved focus. Reports were predominantly positive or mixed, with concerns about overexertion, injury risk, impaired judgment, and fairness in competitive settings. These findings suggest that psychedelics may be understood less as traditional performance-enhancing drugs and more as potential-enhancing substances that alter the subjective conditions under which performance occurs. However, because all accounts were self-reported, it remains unclear whether perceived performance gains correspond to measurable physiological or behavioral improvement. Future controlled research using objective performance outcomes is needed to clarify the relationship between psychedelic use, subjective experience, and physical performance.

articles.title: No single downbeat: Rethinking criteria for beat synchronization across species
articles.authors: Vani G. Rajendran; Hugo Merchant
articles.abstract:
Response to: “Data show that macaques do not synchronize to a beat” by McBride et al., 2026.

McBride et al. claim that our data do not support synchronization to subjective beat. Their argument rests on a series of narrow assumptions: (i) that there exists a single, correct human downbeat, (ii) that there cannot be multiple possible concurrent interpretations of beat, and (iii) that without identifying the phase of this “correct” beat, it is impossible to know whether “macaques are predictively tapping to this subjective beat, or if they are reacting to some salient rhythmic feature.” In essence, McBride et al. seem to have misinterpreted our claim that “macaques can synchronize to a subjective beat in real music” with “macaques synchronize to the human downbeat in real music” - a claim we never make in our article. Nonetheless, it is worthwhile to take this opportunity to critically examine the assumptions that McBride et al’s opinion is based on so that as a field, we can decide whether these assumptions are valid or whether they might require updating.

articles.title: Utility of a Subdomain‑Based ACE‑III Online Dementia Screening Tool
articles.authors: David Foxe; Omar Arnaiz; James Carrick; Sau Chi Cheung; Rebekah Ahmed; Manisha Narasimhan; Yun Tae Hwang; Nicholas J. Cordato; Muireann Irish; Samuel Warren
articles.abstract:
Introduction: The Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination-III (ACE-III) is a widely used dementia screening tool. It provides a total score out of 100 based on five cognitive subdomains: attention, memory, fluency, language, and visuospatial ability. Clinicians often rely on total-score cut-offs (88/100; 82/100) to detect impairment, but patterns across domains may improve diagnostic accuracy. Study aims: (i) develop an optimal model using ACE-III subdomain profiles with probability thresholds; (ii) create an interactive online screening tool.
Methods: Participants were from the FRONTIER Research Group: 431 patients with dementia, 107 controls, yielding 1,068 ACE-III assessments. Logistic regression models were developed and validated in an independent cohort from the Sydney Memory and Ageing Study (64 dementia, 273 controls).
Results: The best model used all five subdomains (Tjur’s R²=0.819), correctly classifying 91.95% of cases at 95% risk threshold, outperforming total-score models (p<0.05).
Discussion: The online ACE-III tool supports personalised dementia risk estimation based on subdomain patterns.

articles.title: One-Day Intracranial Accelerated iTBS: Antidepressant Effects and Mechanistic Insights from a Patient with Major Depression and Epilepsy
articles.authors: Joshua Tatz; Umair Hassan; Joel Berger; Madaline M. Mocchi; Benjamin Pace; Ariane Rhone; Corey Keller; Matthew A. Howard; Aaron Boes; Nicholas Trapp
articles.abstract: Major depression is debilitating and often resistant to pharmacological intervention. Accelerated, transcranial magnetic stimulation protocols involving intermittent theta-burst stimulation (iTBS) to dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC) can produce rapid antidepressant effects, but durability is limited and repeated treatments are often necessary. In the present case study, we evaluated a novel therapeutic strategy involving a 20-session, single-day accelerated iTBS protocol delivered via invasive intracranial stimulation to DLPFC in a patient with depression undergoing intracranial monitoring for treatment-refractory epilepsy. Depression remitted, with symptom reduction persisting for at least nine weeks. Indices of modulation near the DLPFC stimulation site included enhanced theta oscillations in DLPFC, increased single unit firing rates in bilateral hippocampi, and changes in cortico-cortical evoked potentials (CCEPs) in the DLPFC, with early and late components showing different effects. Together, these findings provide proof-of-concept for intracranial iTBS as a therapeutic approach with novel insight into its local and network-level neuromodulatory effects.

articles.title: Collaboration enhances collective intelligence
articles.authors: Belgin Ünal; Aaron Benjamin
articles.abstract: Collective intelligence is revealed on cognitive tasks by the fact that groups typically outperform individuals. Most research on the origin of these benefits focuses on the greater amount and diversity of information collectively held by a group. But group decision-making also involves interactive collaboration, like face-to-face deliberation. The present investigation examined whether brief face-to-face interactions play a substantial role in enhancing collective performance. Across three experiments, participants answered general knowledge questions either individually or in pairs who had the opportunity to discuss and respond jointly. Individual responses were combined using a variety of methods to yield performance metrics for decision-making arising from the aggregation of knowledge in the absence of collaboration. Interacting pairs outperformed all nominal group variants by a substantial margin, indicating that the advantages of teamwork stem not only from the increased knowledge pool possessed by groups, but from the collaborative process of face-to-face interaction itself.

articles.title: Becoming a Psychedelic Therapist:  A Professional Identity Journey
articles.authors: Matthew Pennell; Christine Freer; James Bennett-Levy; Louise Horstmanshof
articles.abstract:
Psychedelic-assisted therapy (PAT) is moving from research settings towards broader implementation. During this transition, the therapist role is being formalised before the developmental pathways into the role are well understood. Existing clinicians are being selected, trained, and authorised to practise in a field marked by an emergent knowledge base and the convergence of multiple cultural and therapeutic traditions. Yet little is known about how therapists come to enter, navigate, and ultimately inhabit the PAT therapist role. This developmental question is central to therapist preparation, but remains undertheorised: what does becoming a PAT therapist involve, how does it unfold, and what supports it? 
This paper draws on contemporary professional identity formation (PIF) theory as a conceptual lens to guide and interpret research on future PAT therapist development. Together with relevant work on psychotherapist development and reflective practice, this lens approaches movement into PAT as a process of professional reconfiguration. It directs attention to what carries forward from prior training, what may need revision, when personal experience becomes a clinical resource rather than unexamined conviction, and how legitimacy is negotiated across diverse practice traditions.
PAT may also stretch existing PIF theory. Unlike many contexts in which PIF has been studied, therapists generally enter PAT as already established practitioners. They also enter a field that lacks a single, coherent identity or community of practice. In addition, some altered-state experiences may change the standpoint from which therapists later reflect on their own development. PAT therapist development may therefore require an expanded account of PIF, capable of theorising formation when both the professional role and the reflecting self remain in flux.

articles.title: Men’s Experiences of Mental Health Services for Depression: Perceptions, Expectations, and Satisfaction
articles.authors: Tsuki Gao; Lesley-Anne MacRae
articles.abstract: Men’s reluctance to seek help for depression persists, with low engagement and high dropout rates in mental health services despite efforts to reduce barriers. Understanding men’s help-seeking journeys is therefore important for improving engagement and access to support. This qualitative study explored men's perceptions, expectations, and satisfaction of mental health support for depression, focusing on how these dimensions interacted over time throughout the help-seeking process. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with seven men aged 21–67 who had experienced depression and engaged with multiple forms of support. Data were analysed using reflexive thematic analysis, generating six themes: (1) The Complexity and Unpredictability of Depression; (2) From Fixing to Facing: Shifting Expectations of Help; (3) Opening the Door to Help-Seeking; (4) Barriers to Help-Seeking; (5) Experiences of Treatment; and (6) Re-imagining Support: Reducing Barriers and Improving Engagement. The findings highlighted the evolution of men’s expectations throughout help-seeking and how different forms of expectation–reality mismatch shaped future engagement. Lack of mental health literacy, stigma, and structural barriers remained problematic, whereas participants identified general practitioners as influential gatekeepers shaping early experiences of support. Within treatment, men demonstrated diverse preferences and emphasised the importance of tailored, responsive support alongside relational fit with practitioners. Men’s engagement with mental health services appeared dynamic, shaped by ongoing interactions between perceptions, expectations, and satisfaction across different stages of help-seeking. The findings suggested that improving mental health literacy, relational responsiveness, personalised care, and service accessibility may be more important than focusing solely on masculine norms. This may inform services that cater for individual differences among men rather than assuming uniform preferences.

articles.title: White Adults’ Verbal and Nonverbal Responses to Discussing Racial Bias: The Role of Bias Awareness
articles.authors: Emily Jade Blevins; Deborah Jean Wu; Allison Louise Skinner; Sylvia Perry
articles.abstract:
Bias awareness—concern about one's propensity to express bias—has been linked to
greater recognition of racial bias, but prior work has relied largely on self-reports. We tested
whether bias awareness predicts observable, real-time responses to interpersonal racism. White
adults (N=123) viewed videos depicting White children engaging in blatant and subtle racism
toward Black peers and then discussed each incident. We coded verbal tentativeness, nonverbal
confidence, and color-conscious and colorblind racial ideology (CBRI) language. Bias awareness
predicted greater behavioral confidence—fewer tentative words and more nonverbal confidence.
Participants responded more confidently and used greater color-conscious and less CBRI
language toward blatant (vs. subtle) racism. Contrary to predictions, bias awareness did not
predict responses to subtle racism, only predicting reduced CBRI language in the blatant
condition. These findings suggest that bias awareness is associated with White individuals’
confidence when discussing racism, though subtle racism is likely downplayed, even among
those higher in bias awareness.

articles.title: The Poker Face Tax: Coherence Between Facial Expression and Subjective Experience as a Predictor of Depression Outcomes in Psychological Treatment
articles.authors: Hadar Fisher; Avigail Bar-Sella; Yael Bouknik; Dennis Kivlighan; sigal zilcha mano
articles.abstract: Emotion coherence, the within-person alignment between subjective emotional experience and outward facial expression, is theorized to serve social-communicative functions, particularly in help-seeking contexts. Despite its theoretical importance, no study has directly tested whether within-session emotion coherence predicts depressive outcomes in psychotherapy. Using multilevel Response Surface Analysis (ML-RSA) in a sample of 65 patients (205 sessions) with major depressive disorder undergoing psychotherapy, we examined whether session-level congruence between self-reported mood and automated facial expression coding predicted subsequent depression scores and whether expressive suppression moderated this effect. Results indicated that greater within-session coherence predicted lower subsequent depressive symptoms, whereas increasing discrepancies between the two were associated with worse outcomes (curvature along the line of incongruence, a₄). Importantly, this effect was directional: sessions characterized by “feeling more than showing”, that is, higher self-reported than facially expressed negative affect, were particularly predictive of worsening depression (slope along the line of incongruence, a₃). These coherence and asymmetry effects were moderated by expressive suppression. Among high suppressors, the link between coherence and lower subsequent depression, as well as the cost of “feeling more than showing”, remained pronounced. In addition, coherence itself became more informative, with sessions marked by jointly elevated reported and expressed negative affect predicted lower subsequent depression, whereas jointly low affect predicted higher subsequent depression (slope along the line of congruence, a₁). These findings carry important theoretical and clinical implications, establishing emotional coherence as a core process in help-seeking environments and a potential therapeutic target, particularly for individuals high in expressive suppression.

articles.title: Does the corollary discharge provide the sensory content of inner speech? A preregistered direct replication and extension of Scott (2013)
articles.authors: Maël Delem; Nina Stauffert; Noel Nguyen; Ursula Debarnot; Aymeric Guillot; Ladislas Nalborczyk
articles.abstract: The mental production of speech or "inner speech" is a remarkable and foundational ability in humans, involved in many daily activities such as reading, writing, remembering, or planning. Inner speech is generally accompanied by a subjective multisensory (mainly auditory) experience. However, the cognitive processes leading to these sensory percepts remain poorly known. One prominent perspective is that the sensory content of inner speech would correspond to the predicted sensory consequences of inhibited speech acts. Scott (2013) provided experimental evidence suggesting that inner speech indeed involves the mental simulation of speech acts, as evidenced by the attenuation of the effect of an external speech sound. However, Scott (2013) assessed silent speech, but not inner speech, which in contrast to silent speech, does not involve visible articulatory movements. The present work aims to replicate and extend the study of Scott (2013) to assess whether inner speech attenuates the perception of concomitant external speech sounds.

articles.title: Domain-specific or executive processing in numerical cognition: The case of decade-unit inversion in Arabic
articles.authors: Dror Dotan; Maha Shamih
articles.abstract: Reading and writing numbers are handled by highly specialized processes dedicated to this purpose. Are domain-general executive processes involved in this task too, at least when it becomes particularly challenging? To address this, we examined a particularly challenging case in a number dictation task: decade-unit inversion. In Arabic (and several other languages), the unit word is said before the decade word (‘five hundred two and thirty’). In a number dictation task, the decades and units must be mentally inverted in order to write 532 from left to right. To cope with the challenge, people sometimes write the units digit before the decade digit, congruent with word order (5, then 2, then 3 in the middle; ‘UD-writing’). Experiment 1 showed that UD-writing is specifically linked to the inversion effort: it was frequent when the auditory decade-unit pair had two words (as in 532) but rare for single-word decade-unit pairs (530, 503). Experiment 2 used a dual-task method to examine the cognitive process underlying inversion. A phonological dual task increased the UD-writing rate, but adding executive load produced no further increase. We conclude that decade-unit inversion relies on phonological, not executive, resources. Specifically, we propose that it relies on the phonological input buffer, a short-term memory store at the verbal input stage, but not on a domain-general working memory system with executive aspects.

articles.title: Item Response Theory Development of a Brief Parenting Measure for Diverse Families: The MAPS V2 Parent-Report
articles.authors: Violeta J. Rodriguez; Mandy Witkin; Jennifer Hellmuth; Justin Parent
articles.abstract:
Abstract
Parenting is a strong predictor of child and caregiver mental health, yet many parenting measures are burdensome or perform unevenly across diverse families. This study developed and validated an ultra-brief parent-report measure derived from the Revised Multidimensional Assessment of Parenting Scale (MAPS V2). Using a national sample of parents of children ages 3–17 (Sample 1; N = 1,699), items were selected with item response theory graded response models to maximize information while minimizing length. The resulting 10-item MAPS V2 Brief Parent-Report assesses Positive Parenting, Hostility, and Lax Control. Confirmatory factor analyses supported a three-factor structure with excellent fit (CFI = .998, TLI = .997, RMSEA = .029, SRMR = .029) and acceptable to good reliability (ω = .75–.81). Criterion-related validity was supported by expected associations with caregiver depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), and child difficulties (SDQ), with Hostility and Lax Control showing stronger links to psychopathology than Positive Parenting. The factor structure and reliability replicated in an independent clinical sample (N = 110), demonstrating excellent fit and comparable reliability (ω = .74–.83). These findings support the MAPS V2 Brief Parent-Report as a psychometrically sound, low-burden measure for research and applied settings.

articles.title: TikTok Disconnection and its Impact on Use, Task Procrastination, and Psychological Well-being in Emerging Adults
articles.authors: Lise-Marie Nassen; Adrian Meier; Kathrin Karsay
articles.abstract: Short-form video platforms like TikTok combine algorithmic personalization with endless scrolling, which fosters immersive use but also raises concerns about procrastination and well-being. Users often attempt to regain control by temporarily disconnecting from TikTok. This preregistered, one-week field experiment (N=196; Mage=22.88) tests whether user agency and disconnection type influence the effectiveness of digital disconnection from TikTok. In a 2×2 between-subjects design, participants were randomly assigned to (a) self-determined vs. predetermined limits and (b) session-length vs. total daily time limits, implemented via TikTok’s native Digital Well-being tools. Across conditions, participants reduced their TikTok use by approximately 20 minutes per day, yet there were no consistent effects of agency or disconnection type. Overall, the intervention modestly improved state-like hedonic and eudaimonic well-being. However, the intervention was insufficient to produce reliable changes in procrastination or broader well-being, suggesting limited effectiveness of built-in disconnection tools such as those offered by TikTok.

articles.title: Misinformation as Strategy: Epistemic Consequences and the Undermining of Shared Truth
articles.authors: Ullrich K. H. Ecker; Emily Rebecca Spearing; Renee DiResta; Stephan Lewandowsky
articles.abstract: Misinformation influences cognition—shaping memory, beliefs, attitudes, reasoning, and decision making. While intentionally disseminated misinformation (i.e., disinformation) has long served persuasive functions, its strategic use to generate epistemic uncertainty has grown in prominence. Such strategic manipulation by incentivised actors serves to construct “alternative realities” that undermine shared truth and institutional trust. This paper reviews recent evidence on the direct cognitive and behavioural impacts of misinformation, examines the transformative roles of social media and generative artificial intelligence, and considers broader epistemological consequences, including the erosion of truth norms and implications for democratic discourse. Addressing these challenges requires coordinated individual and systemic interventions that integrate psychological, technological, educational, and regulatory approaches to strengthen epistemic resilience and safeguard evidence-informed public reasoning and decision-making.

articles.title: A Beta Way: A Tutorial on Bayesian Beta Regression for Psychological Research
articles.authors: Jason Geller; Robert Kubinec; Chelsea Parlett; Matti Vuorre
articles.abstract: Rates, percentages, and proportions are common outcomes in psychology and the social sciences. These outcomes are often analyzed using models that assume normality, but this practice overlooks important features of the data, such as their natural bounds at 0 and 1. As a result, estimates can become distorted. In contrast, treating such outcomes as Beta-distributed respects these limits and can yield more accurate estimates. Despite these advantages, the use of Beta models in applied research remains limited. Our goal is to provide researchers with practical guidance for adopting Beta regression models, illustrated with an example drawn from the psychological literature. We begin by introducing the Beta distribution and Beta regression, emphasizing key components and assumptions. Next, using data from a learning and memory study, we demonstrate how to fit a Beta regression model in R with the Bayesian package `brms` and how to interpret results on the response scale. We also discuss model extensions, including zero-inflated, zero- and one-inflated, and ordered Beta models. Basic familarity with regression modeling and R is assumed. To promote wider adoption of these methods, we provide detailed code and materials at https://github.com/jgeller112/Beta_regression_tutorial.

articles.title: Families of young people who self-harm: A mixed-methods systematic review of their experiences and needs
articles.authors: Beatriz Cação-Dias; Inês Areal Rothes; Cláudia Carmo; Cristina Nunes; Marta Brás
articles.abstract: Background: Self-harm in young people is a pressing public health issue, with family support playing a crucial role in the young person’s prognosis. Concurrently, the impact extends to families themselves, who must navigate caregiving responsibilities while also requiring support. Understanding these experiences is key to providing more effective assistance in their caregiving roles. Aim: This mixed-methods systematic review aims to investigate the experiences, barriers, and needs of families of young people who self-harm. Method: Following PRISMA guidelines, a search was conducted across PsycINFO, PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus databases in July 2024 and April 2025. A total of 31 studies were included in the review, and quality was assessed using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool. A narrative synthesis was employed for the quantitative data, while qualitative data were analysed using thematic synthesis. Results: The quantitative findings revealed two themes: (1) the repercussions of self-harm in the family, and (2) family support needs. Qualitative analysis identified four themes: (1) parents’ emotional and psychological impact; (2) impact on parenting and bond with young person; (3) disruption in wider family dynamics; and (4) barriers to and pathways for support. Conclusion: The review discusses the implications of these findings, offering recommendations for future research and improvements in family support services to alleviate caregiver burden and foster supportive environments for recovery.

articles.title: Peer support in employment services for people living with psychotic disorders
articles.authors: Salma Bachir Cherif; Genevieve Sauve; Amal Abdel-Baki; Srividya Iyer; Chad Chouinard; Simon Longpré; Tania Leduc
articles.abstract:
Background
Psychotic disorders are associated with significant barriers to employment. Even if work can support recovery and improve social functioning, most people living with psychotic disorders remain unemployed. Supported employment programs help these individuals integrate the job market, but we observe a lack of documentation about peer support interventions in those services. This paper presents the protocol for a study aimed at exploring the underlying factors through which peer support may influence employment among people living with psychotic disorders within the socio-cultural and economic context of the province of Quebec, Canada. 
Methods
This qualitative research was developed in collaboration with a specialized community organization using a participatory approach. Workshops were led by peer workers, addressing different themes such as coping with illness, disclosure, and social skills. Data is still collected through focus groups with three categories of stakeholders: (1) participants, (2) peer workers and (3) employment counselors. Data will be analyzed using Braun and Clarke’s inductive thematic analysis, supported by NVivo, with member checking and reflective journaling to strengthen rigor.
Discussion
This study is expected to contribute to an emerging area of research by documenting how peer support intervention may help promote recovery by improving employability for people living with psychotic disorders. Results are expected to highlight how lived experience, as an expertise, can play a role and complement employment services when it comes to accessing the job market as a person living with psychotic disorders. This research could help develop a more inclusive, community-based, and scalable employment programs and services. 
Keywords
Peer support, severe mental illness, psychotic disorders, employability, work

articles.title: A Compression-Based Account of Biases Towards Simpler Explanations
articles.authors: Thalia Vrantsidis
articles.abstract:
People often prefer simpler explanations, sometimes even beyond what is justified by 
available probability information, creating a form of simplicity bias. This work investigates the 
source and nature of such simplicity biases. Across three studies and four supplementary studies, 
the current work contrasts the dominant Bayesian account, where an explanation’s structural 
simplicity (number of root causes) is used as a cue to its prior probability, with an alternative 
compression-based account, which emphasizes the key role of cognitive simplicity (ease of 
processing, or processing fluency). Results consistently support the latter view. Simplicity biases 
were closely tied to processing ease, emerging from both spontaneous variation (Study 1) and 
from manipulations of explanations’ clarity and familiarity (Study 2), even when holding the 
number of causes constant. Simplicity biases also arose directly in posterior probability 
judgments, rather than in priors—consistent with a compression-based view, if the main 
processing difficulty arises when linking complex explanations with their effects—while priors 
instead showed a complexity bias, due to overestimating joint probabilities. Study 3 shed light on 
the functions of these simplicity biases, suggesting that ease of processing is seen both as a cue 
to an explanation’s probability, and as a valued feature in its own right. Together, these findings 
suggest that biases towards simpler explanations reflect general mechanisms related to 
processing ease, rather than a distinct role for structural simplicity, and highlight the importance 
of considering cognitive processes, not just abstract properties of explanations, in order to 
understand both mechanisms and biases in explanatory reasoning.

articles.title: Mathematical Relationships Between Brains and Consciousness
articles.authors: Alexander Maier
articles.abstract: A chief aim of the neuroscience of consciousness is to derive how states of conscious experience (i.e., qualia) are related to supporting brain states (e.g., patterns of neural activation within patterns of neural connectivity). This aim goes beyond identifying neuronal correlates of consciousness (NCC) on the one side, and beyond structural descriptions of conscious experience (e.g., qualia structure) on the other side. The ultimate goal is to link these two sides by mapping specific neuronal states or mechanisms to specific conscious experiences. Relating two or more phenomena in science often takes the form of mathematical mappings, such as equations (e.g., E = mc^2). This raises the possibility that there also are mathematical mappings that relate conscious states to brain states. Here we review empirical work on mapping neuronal spiking activity to conscious contents. We specifically contrast pioneering paradigms aimed at identifying “bridge loci”, in the form of NCC, with paradigms aimed at identifying “bridge laws”, such as relating psychometric to neurometric functions. We then examine how these paradigms are augmented by mapping structural descriptions of conscious experience to structural descriptions of neuronal activity.

articles.title: From Thought to Senses: Assessing the Relationship Between the Generation Effect and Multisensory Facilitation
articles.authors: Michaela Ritchie; Jonathan Wilbiks
articles.abstract: The current study investigated whether the generation effect, a memory advantage for self-generated verbal information, is enhanced in multisensory conditions. Such a finding would be consistent with the multisensory facilitation effect, a phenomenon wherein multiple sensory inputs may reduce the cognitive load required to process and respond to co-occurring stimuli from multiple senses. Although extensive research has explored the generation effect, the multisensory aspect of this task has been overlooked. In the current study, we aimed to determine if the generation effect is modulated by the multisensory facilitation effect. Multisensory facilitation involves the brain’s ability to efficiently process information from diverse sensory sources simultaneously, enhancing cognitive performance. We employed a 2 (Task Type: generate, read) X 3 (Sensory Modality: auditory, visual, multisensory) factorial design, utilizing word pair lists and a cued recall test. A generation effect emerged for the auditory and audiovisual condition, but not for the visual condition. The hypothesis that the magnitude of the generation effect would be greater for the multisensory encoding condition was not supported. Overall, the hypothesis that generation effect will be enhanced for multisensory learning was not supported. The current study addresses a gap in existing literature by examining the multisensory component of generation tasks and extends the current understanding of multisensory learning by incorporating verbal stimuli as the visual component.

articles.title: Being on track in early numeracy and literacy: A national study of South African preschoolers
articles.authors: Parvin Nemati; Gabrielle Garon-Carrier; Annie Lemieux; Elizabeth Henning; Mojtaba Soltanlou
articles.abstract:
In South Africa, 58% of preschoolers enter school unprepared, a proportion nearly double that of many European and North American countries. Identifying the contributing factors to this gap in school readiness is a pressing public and policy priority. Data from the Thrive by Five Index 2024, a nationally representative survey of 5,001 South African preschoolers (Mean age = 54.8 months; 51% girls) were used to examine associations between child-level characteristics, home learning environment, preschool programs, and the likelihood of being “on-track” for early numeracy and literacy skills as core foundations of school readiness, using the Early Learning Outcomes Measure (ELOM 4&5).
Multivariate logistic regression revealed child-level characteristics, including cognitive, fine motor, emotional, and attentional skills, were the most salient predictors, explaining substantial variance in outcomes (Numeracy R²=26.4%; Literacy R²=25.0%). No home learning environment measures, including caregiver’s background and parental stimulation retained statistical significance. At the preschool level, better planning and assessment was associated with literacy readiness, while positive relationships and interactions were unexpectedly associated with lower numeracy skills. Findings underscore the multidimensional nature of school readiness in South Africa, advocating for strengthening the child’s developmental capacities alongside investing in home learning environment and the quality of the preschool programs.

articles.title: Automating abstract screening in research synthesis using large language models: A tutorial and proof-of-concept study
articles.authors: Mirka Henninger; Jan Radek; Jean-Paul Snijder; Martin Pauly
articles.abstract: Screening abstracts is a crucial yet labor-intensive step in research synthesis projects such as systematic reviews and meta-analyses. Large language models (LLMs) promise an opportunity to streamline and automate this process. However, there is still little guidance and practical insight into how such automated workflows can be implemented in research practice. In this article, we propose, describe, and illustrate an abstract screening procedure using LLMs including a quantification of uncertainty of model outputs. We provide a step-by-step tutorial in which we address how to select an LLM, access LLMs from within R, develop and refine suitable prompts, define structured output formats, and whether and how model hyperparameters should be set. We then illustrate this procedure in a proof-of-concept study using a set of human-rated abstracts from a recent meta-analysis for comparison. We demonstrate that LLM-assisted screening can substantially reduce the time and cost of review preparation while maintaining accuracy comparable to human raters. To support psychological researchers in adopting LLM-based workflows for research synthesis, we also provide R scripts and implementation guidance on Gitlab. At the same time, we emphasize that this work represents an initial step, and that continued refinement and validation are essential as LLM technologies and their applications continue to evolve rapidly.

articles.title: Declining Ethnic Dominance and Democratic Quality Across Six Decades and 90 Countries
articles.authors: Lucas Woodley; Joshua D. Greene
articles.abstract: Does diversity strengthen democracy or weaken it? And what kind of diversity matters? In the past 80 years, nations have become, on average, both more ethnically diverse and more democratic, suggesting a positive relationship between diversity and democracy. However, diversity creates the potential for intergroup conflict, which can weaken democracy. Consistent with this, resurgent ethnic nationalism featuring opposition to immigration and concerns about the status of dominant groups has accompanied democratic backsliding. Here we examine the relationship between diversity and democracy by combining complementary data from six historical data sets covering 90 countries from 1950 to 2010. We use multiple measures of democratic quality and track diversity using a conventional measure (fractionalization) as well as a measure of ethnic polarization and, critically, the historically dominant group’s share of the population. Using dynamic panel models, we show that declines in ethnic dominance predict significant reductions in electoral, liberal, egalitarian, and participatory democracy. Crucially, we find that these declines are typically followed by periods of democratic resilience, suggesting that democratic backsliding is reversible and that declining ethnic dominance may lead to stronger democratic institutions over time. These findings may provide insights for fortifying democratic institutions in increasingly diverse societies.

articles.title: Effects of Process- and Embodiment-Focused Psychology (PEP) Treatment for Depression, comorbid Anxiety and Generalized Self-Efficacy: A Quasi-Experimental Pilot Study
articles.authors: Laura Grabener; Can Kulens; Anne Martinelli
articles.abstract:
Background
Depressive and anxiety disorders are highly prevalent and frequently co-occur, highlighting the need for accessible and
low-threshold treatment approaches. Process- and Embodiment-Focused Psychology (PEP) is an integrative intervention
combining cognitive techniques with embodiment-focused procedures such as sensorimotor self-stimulation and
structured work with self-referential cognitions. To date, however, no controlled studies have systematically examined
the effects of therapist-guided PEP treatment on depressive symptoms, anxiety symptoms, and generalized self-efficacy.
The present pilot study therefore investigated whether PEP treatment is associated with changes in these outcomes
compared to a literature-based information condition.

Methods
Twenty-five adults (12 experimental group, 13 control group; age range 18–58 years, M = 43.48, SD = 11.51) with
depressive symptoms and in part with comorbid anxiety symptoms (n = 15), participated in a quasi-experimental
longitudinal study with two assessment time points. Participants received either therapist-guided PEP treatment (EG) or
engaged in structured self-study using a PEP-based self-help manual (CG) over approximately six weeks. Depressive
symptoms (BDI-II), anxiety symptoms (BAI), and generalized self-efficacy (GSWE) were assessed at baseline and post-
intervention. Mixed-design repeated-measures ANOVAs were conducted to examine time × group interaction effects.

Results
Significant improvements over time were observed for depressive symptoms, F(1,23) = 121.15, p < .001, η²p = .84,
anxiety symptoms, F(1,13) = 89.08, p < .001, η²p = .87, and generalized self-efficacy, F(1,23) = 41.97, p < .001, η²p = .65.
Significant time × group interaction effects indicated stronger symptom reductions and larger increases in generalized
self-efficacy in the therapist-guided PEP condition (BDI-II: F(1,23) = 27.23, p < .001, η²p = .54; BAI: F(1,13) = 323.00, p
< .001, η²p = .96; GSWE: F(1,23) = 5.51, p = .028, η²p = .19). Within-group effect sizes were large in the experimental
group (BDI-II: d = 3.05; BAI: d = 2.31; GSWE: d = 1.93). In the control group, within-group effect sizes were also large for
depressive and anxiety symptoms and moderate-to-large for generalized self-efficacy (BDI-II: d = 1.24; BAI: d = 1.24;
GSWE: d = 0.77).

Conclusions
The findings provide preliminary evidence that therapist-guided PEP treatment may contribute to reductions in
depressive and anxiety symptoms and to increases in generalized self-efficacy. Although causal conclusions are limited
due to the quasi-experimental design, baseline group differences, and small sample size, the results support the
potential clinical relevance of PEP as an integrative and resource-oriented intervention approach. Further randomized
controlled studies are needed to confirm these findings.

articles.title: “You’re playing the game wrong”: Exploring Motivations and Perceptions of Save-scumming in Video Games.
articles.authors: George Farmer; Danielle Kelly; Amanda Jones-Rincon
articles.abstract: The practice of ‘save-scumming’ is an under-researched phenomenon within media psychology. While save-scumming allows players in a role-playing game to reverse the consequences of a regrettable decision by reloading a save state, its use has sparked widespread debate among video gamers over whether it is morally acceptable. Therefore, it is of academic and lay interest to explore the nuances of save-scumming through qualitative research interviews with video game players. Utilising reflexive thematic analysis through the lens of self-determination theory, UK and US participants described motivational factors that encourage save-scumming and its benefits. This exploratory study provides a theoretical basis for future investigations and contributes to a more comprehensive understanding of motivations for save-scumming behaviour, which has implications for the study of video games and broader gaming culture.

articles.title: A group-based prevention program fostering mental health and physical activity: Pre- to post-program outcomes and session-level measures
articles.authors: Mareike Röttger; Jens Blechert; Matthias Burkard Aulbach
articles.abstract:
Objective: Physical activity (PA) and psychoeducational sessions (PsycEd) represent popular and effective components of well-being-oriented prevention programs. However, while program-level (distal) improvements are well-documented, less is known about session-level (proximal) processes and their relation to overall program effects. 
Methods: We investigated an 8-week, naturalistic, non-controlled, health-insurance-provided group-based prevention program comprising weekly outdoor PA and PsycEd in individuals often experiencing low mood, energy levels, tiredness, or irritability. As proximal outcomes, 80 participants reported their current mood and stress (time pressure and burden) before and after session times. They further rated each session on social support, group atmosphere, and helpfulness. Engagement was operationalized as session attendance. Changes in affective well-being (WHO-5) were assessed as the program-level, distal effect. Its moderation by session-level measures were analysed.  
Results: WHO-5 scores improved significantly from pre- to post-program (d=.25). Significant proximal improvements in mood (d>.60) and stress burden (d>.20) were found for PsyEd and PA. Only PA sessions were significantly linked with time pressure reductions (d=.17), while those sessions were also rated higher on social support (d=.65), group atmosphere (d=.49), and helpfulness (d=0.32). There was no significant difference between session types in attendance. Participants attending more sessions had higher WHO-5 improvements (β=.18), but no other session-level variable significantly moderated this improvement. 
Conclusion: While the study’s limitations of the missing control group, the potential mismatch between distal and proximal measures, and the mostly female sample should be considered, potential program effectiveness might be boosted by targeting continued attendance. Outdoor PA might be slightly superior to PsycEd in activating group benefits and could potentially be expanded.

articles.title: Media Education in Residential Child and Youth Welfare –  Assessment of the Individual and Institutional Status Quo
articles.authors: Marvin Fendt; Marwa Toumiat; Vanessa Fuß; Lisa von Glischinski
articles.abstract: Mediatization presents residential child and youth care with the challenge of fostering digital participation while also protecting clients from risks. Despite high demands on pedagogical professionals, empirical insights into the status quo of media education in these settings remain scarce. This project aimed to assess predictors for professional media education and to contrast the perspectives of professionals with those of the adolescents in their care. Across three studies, we surveyed professionals and adolescents in German residential care institutions. Study 1 (N = 73) and Study 3 (N = 140) analyzed predictors of active and restrictive mediation strategies. Study 2 (N = 80) and a subsample of Study 3 compared youth and professional perspectives on mediation and risk perception. We found that worse professional media literacy predicted more restrictive mediation, active mediation was strongly predicted by individual motivation and daily media pedagogical habits, and a more intense institutional focus on media pedagogy and higher age predicted both. We found notable discrepancies between professional and youth perspectives on the intensity of mediation and the prevalence of digital risks. Nonetheless, we found that institutional conditions for media pedagogy were highly resource-dependent and inconsistent. We therefore sketch an outline for a future professionalization framework, focusing on structural enablement, pedagogical attitude, and methodological competence to provide sustainable professional development.

articles.title: Childhood Predictors of Educational Attainment Across 22 Countries: A Cross-National Analysis of the Global Flourishing Study
articles.authors: Roza Nalbandyan; R. Noah Padgett; Christina Hinton; Merve Cerit; Byron R. Johnson; Tyler J. VanderWeele
articles.abstract: Educational attainment is a key determinant of adult flourishing, yet the childhood experiences associated with higher attainment have been studied predominantly in Western societies and typically in isolation. This study examined associations between 11 retrospectively assessed childhood predictors and adult educational attainment across 22 diverse countries (N = 202,898) using data from the Global Flourishing Study. Country-specific modified Poisson regression models were pooled using random-effects and population weighted meta-analysis, with E-value sensitivity analyses to assess robustness to unmeasured confounding. Subjective financial status of the family growing up was the strongest and most consistent predictor of completing 16 or more years of education that was directionally consistent across virtually all countries, but the magnitude of association varied across countries. Religious service attendance at age 12, feeling like an outsider in one's family, self-rated childhood health, and parental marital status were also associated with educational attainment, with moderate robustness to unmeasured confounding. Parental relationship quality and childhood abuse showed near-null pooled associations after mutual adjustment. Immigration status and gender showed highly heterogeneous associations whose direction depended on national context; the pooled female-to-male risk ratio was 0.92 (95% CI: 0.81, 1.03) under random-effects but 0.75 (95% CI: 0.66, 0.85) under population-weighted meta-analysis. These patterns were broadly consistent when high-school equivalence was used as an alternative outcome. These findings underscore the cross-national consistency of socioeconomic and sociocultural factors in education and the context-dependence of other childhood experiences.

articles.title: Brevity is the soul of wit. Developing Parallel Short Tests for Planetary Health  Knowledge
articles.authors: Franziska Rees; Oliver Wilhelm
articles.abstract:
Planetary Health Knowledge (PHK)—knowledge about human health, environmental 
systems, societal systems, and their interactions—is a prerequisite for global citizenship. We 
developed three parallel ultra-short PHK tests (14 items each), covering all seven PHK 
domains, using ant colony optimization for parallel test assembly. Items were drawn from a 
pool of 175 multiple-choice items administered to N = 934 US participants in a balanced-
incomplete-block-design. Highly comparable test-characteristic-curves and test-information-
functions supported parallelism across test versions, which showed excellent model fit and 
moderate reliability. We further introduce the Reversed Adjusted Overlap Index (RAOI) and 
normalized Shannon entropy to evaluate quality of the underlying item pool, indicating high 
psychometric quality and relative homogeneity. PHK was negatively associated with 
hierarchical and dominance-oriented worldviews and positively associated with climate 
change beliefs. The parallel PHK test versions allow for efficient PHK assessment also in 
longitudinal and intervention studies and are openly available in English and German.

articles.title: Skewed and Balanced Input in Primary 2 School L2 Construction Learning
articles.authors: Johannes Schulz; Katja Schwemmer; Katharina Loibl
articles.abstract:
Laboratory-based artificial language studies suggest that skewed input
supports children’s construction learning by reducing analogical reasoning
demands through increased surface similarity. Natural language L2
studies find no such effect, potentially because crosslinguistic similarity
pre-empts it. We argue that those studies resembled laboratory
conditions without reproducing the cognitive demands of authentic
classrooms. Therefore, we investigated whether skew effects re-emerge
under increased cognitive demands in authentic classrooms and whether
sequencing input distributions affects learning.
In a randomized-controlled trial (n = 122), German Year 4 students (9-
10-year-olds) learning English received either skewed followed by
balanced input or balanced input throughout. While teaching yielded
robust learning of the target approach-event construction, intermediate
and post-test data revealed evidence against a skew effect and no
evidence of a sequencing effect across phases. These findings extend
previous child L2 research by demonstrating null effects of skewing even
in authentic classrooms, challenging the effect’s relevance in L2 pedagogy.

articles.title: The Adaptive Role of Pre-Service Routines in Tennis: An Ecological Dynamics Approach to Self-Regulation in Sports Tasks
articles.authors: Adriano Carvalho; João Carvalho; Ana Diniz; Henrique Brito; Henrique Lopes; Daniel Carrilho; Margarida Vaz de Carvalho; Catarina Belchior; Duarte Araújo
articles.abstract:
Pre-performance routines (PPR) can be defined as the set of behaviours and thoughts systematically used to prepare sport tasks execution. How consistently athletes should perform their PPR, is a topic with contradictory findings and interpretations. Guided by an ecological dynamics approach, we propose that self-regulatory action temporarily emerges from the exploration of several constraints (i.e., task, environment, and athlete), implying that PPR are an adaptive process that deals with performance circumstances, supporting and allowing task goal achievement.
Using the “behaviour setting” concept, we applied a “behavioural mapping” technique to conduct an observational study on 10 professional players (i.e., ranked from 1º to 744º at the Association of Tennis Professionals), and described their pre-service routines (PSR) components (i.e., type of behaviours, their frequency, and duration) for a full official match. A total of 611 PSR were observed. Results indicate that PSR are subject of contextually constrained functional variability. Specifically, the length of the previous point explains from 23% to 47% the adaptation of PSR time, and different contextual challenges (i.e., critical points, result of the previous point, and set score) promoted individualized PSR adaptations (p < .05). Results suggest that PSR in tennis are shaped by interacting constraints, requiring athletes to functionally adapt their self-regulatory behaviour to deal with performance circumstances. These findings present important practical implications for PSR prescription, suggesting that, instead of being a rigid script of thoughts and behaviours, PSR development should allow athletes to functionally adapt their routines for each specific sport task to be executed.

articles.title: Event boundaries impair immediate and delayed story recall in healthy aging and Mild Cognitive Impairment
articles.authors: Flavia De Luca; Annabel Rowney-Smith; Naji Tabet; Alan Garnham; Chris Bird
articles.abstract: Changes in a narrative storyline are perceived as event boundaries and are associated with increased attention to the story and better memory for sections of the narrative where the changes occur. However, the effect of event boundaries on overall memory for a story is unclear. Here we employed a new naturalistic task in which we manipulated the number of event boundaries in short stories. We tested story recall at two retention intervals (immediate, one-hour delay), in two participant groups (healthy adults, adults with Mild Cognitive Impairment, MCI). Despite lower overall performance in the MCI group, the findings were consistent across retention intervals and groups; the addition of an event boundary resulted in poorer story recall. Importantly, this effect was driven by "all-or-none" forgetting of the event following a boundary. We suggest that event boundaries induce a contextual shift that disrupts the causal structure of a narrative and weakens the binding of story elements. As a result, recalling one event is less likely to cue the retrieval of the subsequent event, compared with recalling elements that cue the retrieval of successive details from the same event. Thus, overall, stories that comprise fewer longer events will be remembered better than stories that comprise more shorter events.

articles.title: The Limits of Identifiability in the Self-Exciting Ornstein–Uhlenbeck Model
articles.authors: Hannah Pongratz; Constantin Gregor Meyer-Grant
articles.abstract:
Evidence-accumulation models play a central role in the field of decision-making research, offering a comprehensive framework for understanding choice behavior and response times, including speed–accuracy tradeoffs (SATs). In this framework, the dominant explanation for SATs has long been that prioritizing speed lowers the boundary separation, meaning less evidence is needed to make a choice. Recently, however, an alternative account has been proposed according to which emphasizing speed induces a confirmation bias toward the currently favored option. Such a behavior can be formally captured by a self-exciting Ornstein–Uhlenbeck process. However, drawing on asymptotic arguments and extensive simulation studies, we demonstrate that under certain conditions, key parameters of the resulting evidence-accumulation model are not jointly identifiable. We explain the underlying cause of this identifiability issue, examine its practical consequences for model-based inference, and
present potential strategies to resolve or mitigate it.

articles.title: Corruption Cues Increase Conspiracy Beliefs: The Central Role of Perceived Distributive Fairness
articles.authors: Sinan Alper; Onurcan Yilmaz
