articles.abstract: Perceptions of institutional corruption are robustly associated with conspiracy beliefs, yet the evidence is largely correlational and silent on the perceptions that link them. Across two preregistered experiments with nationally representative US samples, we manipulated the institutional corruption to test how it fosters conspiratorial thinking. In Study 1 (N = 1,103), framing an unnamed US state as highly corrupt increased endorsement of conspiratorial allegations. This increase was accounted for by perceptions that wrongdoers go unpunished, that resources are distributed unfairly, and that institutions provide unreliable information, with distributive fairness showing the strongest association. Study 2 (N = 750) replicated the pattern with a fictional country and showed that corruption raised belief in both plausible and implausible conspiracies, although the indirect associations through fairness and information emerged only for plausible ones. Corruption thus broadly heightens conspiratorial suspicion, whereas its link to realistic allegations is carried by specific, theoretically meaningful institutional perceptions.

articles.title: Developing LGBTQ+ Acceptance: Media as Resources for LGBTQ+ People and Close Others
articles.authors: Joël Hendrix; Marieke Fransen; Gijsbert Bijlstra; Serena Daalmans
articles.abstract: Discrimination against LGBTQ+ people is increasing, and acceptance is declining. Prior research has focused on supporting LGBTQ+ individuals in developing their identity and self-acceptance, and on increasing LGBTQ+ acceptance among non-LGBTQ+ people. To examine media's roles in these processes, we studied how media serve as resources for LGBTQ+ self- and other acceptance. We conducted 31 semi-structured interviews with LGBTQ+ individuals and close others (e.g., friends, family). Through thematic analysis, we identified a pattern of resources that positioned media within a broader context. We also identified six motivations for media use that fulfill eight needs in developing LGBTQ+ acceptance. There was significant overlap in media use among LGBTQ+ and close others. We discuss implications, limitations, and future research directions.

articles.title: Bayesian Efficient Coding as a Theory of Perception: Progress, Controversies and Prospects
articles.authors: Ivan Tomic; Máté Lengyel; Paul Bays
articles.abstract: Bayesian efficient coding unifies two foundational theories of sensory processing: efficient coding and Bayesian inference. Central to this account is the idea that natural environmental statistics shape both how sensory information is encoded and how it is perceptually interpreted. By unifying these principles, the framework accounts for counterintuitive perceptual biases and establishes lawful relationships between environmental statistics, bias, and discrimination thresholds. Here, we review behavioural and neural evidence for this theory in perception and cognition, as well as how short- and long-term adaptation to the environment may be expressed within the framework. We further review theoretical developments that extend the original framework, focusing on how response biases can be decomposed into encoding- and decoding-related components. A decade after its introduction, Bayesian efficient coding continues to evolve as a powerful theory, with recent extensions addressing early limitations and opening new directions for investigating perception and cognition.

articles.title: Baselines Matter: Cardiovascular Baseline Procedures in Affective Psychophysiology
articles.authors: Maciej Behnke; Wadim Leszek Krzyżaniak; Szymon Jęśko-Białek; Patryk Andrzej Maciejewski; Audrey-Ann Journault; Sylvia D Kreibig; Jeremy Jamieson; James Gross
articles.abstract: A baseline is necessary to define biological reactivity in psychophysiological research. However, the conceptualization and implementation of baseline measurements are often underdescribed and weakly justified. This poses a problem because poor baseline decisions have the potential to introduce noise, reduce comparability across studies, and bias interpretations of data. In this article, we focus on laboratory studies using cardiovascular measures to examine how baselines are described and used in affective psychophysiology. To describe heterogeneity in baseline procedures, Study 1 reviewed 310 psychophysiological studies and coded baseline activities, durations, analyzed intervals, and theoretical justifications. To examine within-baseline stabilization, Study 2 analyzed the POPANE dataset to assess minute-by-minute changes in cardiovascular activity in 1,010 participants during a standard 5-minute baseline. To test whether these patterns replicated and whether baseline estimates differed across sessions, Study 3 analyzed the CEPAV dataset in 300 participants across two separate laboratory visits. Finally, to assess generalizability across a larger and more heterogeneous sample, Study 4 replicated these analyses using compiled laboratory data from 2,395 participants. Across studies, results indicate that baseline procedures vary considerably, are often insufficiently reported, and may meaningfully impact the interpretation of cardiovascular data. Although different cardiovascular indices may stabilize differently during a 5-minute baseline, findings suggest that this procedure is a reasonable, pragmatic choice and that separate-session baselines should not be assumed to be superior. More broadly, findings highlight the need for stronger justification, standardization, and reporting of baseline procedures in psychophysiological research.

articles.title: Relational Mobility and the Sharing of Positive and Negative Reputation Across Societies
articles.authors: Haruno Kusakabe; Masaki Yuki
articles.abstract: The sharing and use of reputational information about others are fundamental to human society. However, little is known about how such communication varies across socio-ecological conditions. Here, we examine how relational mobility, a socio-ecological factor representing the degree of freedom and opportunity individuals have to form and leave interpersonal relationships, shapes the sharing of reputational information in society. We conducted three cross-societal surveys in the United States and Japan (total N = 930). Participants reported their perceptions of how positive and negative reputational information is exchanged in their society (Studies 1a, 1b, and 2) and, in Study 2, their own communication of positive and negative reputational information in everyday life. Across studies, relational mobility was positively associated with the perception and communication of positive reputational information and negatively associated with the perception and communication of negative reputational information. These findings highlight the importance of considering the socio-ecological contexts in which reputational information is produced and exchanged when studying reputation-based psychological processes and behaviours, including cooperation.

articles.title: The anatomy of happiness: Dissecting differential effects of education on material life satisfaction and relationship satisfaction in the UK Biobank
articles.authors: Chris Patrick Pflanz; Sarah Bauermeister; John Gallacher
articles.abstract:
Background. There is a known association between education and subjective well-being. However, research found that higher levels of education were associated with both higher and lower levels of subjective well-being in various populations. These prima-facie counter-intuitive findings may arise because subjective well-being may be too broad a construct with a plethora of contributing factors. Furthermore, confounding variables may explain the putative association between education and subjective well-being, therefore necessitating a thorough adjustment thereof. Confounding variables comprise, inter alia, cardiometabolic health, socioeconomic factors, psychosocial lifestyle variables, and mental health.
Objective. To investigate the effect of education on subjective well-being in the UK Biobank whilst controlling for confounding variables. To investigate differential effects of education on material life satisfaction and relationship satisfaction.
Methods. Exploratory factor analysis was conducted to reveal the underlying factor structure of the UK Biobank happiness questionnaire. Serial regression analyses were performed to elucidate the association between education and subjective well-being whilst iteratively adding confounding variables into the model. 
Results. Education evinced a prima-facie counter-intuitive negative effect on subjective well-being. Exploratory factor analysis indicated that subjective well-being may be decomposed into material life satisfaction and relationship satisfaction. Education differentially affected these constructs; wherein higher levels of education were associated with more material life satisfaction but lower levels of relationship satisfaction, even after thorough adjustment of confounding variables. 
Conclusion. The effects of education on subjective well-being are complex with evidence for increased material satisfaction but reduced relationship satisfaction. The presupposition that subjective wellbeing can be adequately captured with a single construct has been shattered.  Further research should investigate the effect of education on domains of subjective wellbeing rather than amalgamating domains into too broad a construct to be informative.

articles.title: Differential item-functioning in the Eysenck neuroticism scale and its implications on the concurrent and predictive validity of the neuroticism construct
articles.authors: Chris Patrick Pflanz; Sarah Bauermeister; John D Gallacher
articles.abstract:
Background. Gender differences in neuroticism are well known, with females consistently scoring higher than males across various scales, including the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire-Revised Neuroticism (EPQ-RN) scale. However, it remains unclear whether these differences are genuine or result from gender bias in psychometric instruments. Additionally, the implications of a potential bias for the predictive validity of neuroticism are not well understood. Given the role of neuroticism in predicting cognitive performance, this study aimed to investigate gender-based differential item functioning (DIF) in the EPQ-RN and its effects on the association between neuroticism and cognition.
Methods. Using data from 13,573 participants in the UK Biobank study, we conducted logistic regression DIF analyses on the EPQ-RN scale to identify gender-related item bias. Items were grouped into three constructs: bias-free, female-endorsed, and male-endorsed neuroticism. Cross-sectional linear regression and longitudinal structural equation modelling (SEM) were used to evaluate the association between neuroticism constructs and cognition, adjusting for age, gender, education, and depression.
Results. Significant DIF was identified in 10 of 12 EPQ-RN items, with six preferentially endorsed by females. However, females also scored higher on bias-free neuroticism, indicating actual gender differences. In cross-sectional analyses, bias-free and female neuroticism were significantly associated with poorer cognitive performance at baseline, whereas the effect of male neuroticism was conditional on the covariates in the model. Longitudinal SEMs did not reveal significant cross-lagged effects, suggesting neuroticism at baseline did not predict cognitive performance at follow-up, irrespective of the type of neuroticism subscale: bias-free, female, or male. 
Conclusion. Findings reveal substantial gender-based DIF in the EPQ-RN scale, likely overestimating neuroticism scores in females. Yet, even after removing biased items, females still exhibited higher bias-free neuroticism, supporting the notion of genuine gender differences. Importantly, the validity of the neuroticism construct as a predictor of cognition did not depend on DIF, with similar findings for bias-free and gender-specific scales.  These findings have implications for interpreting gender differences in cognitive aging, particularly when using widely adopted personality scales such as the EPQ-RN.
Keywords: Neuroticism, cognition, longitudinal, gender effects, gender bias, epidemiological research, lifespan development, psychometrics, differential item functioning.

articles.title: Natural Language Processing (NLP) based verbal fluency markers as differential diagnostic indicators of early psychotic, affective and clinical high-risk states
articles.authors: Millenia Chakraborty; Julian Wenzel; Louisa Schwed; Felix Menne; Alexandra König; Annkathrin Böke; Hannah Hacker; Carolina Bonivento; Alexandra Stainton; Dominic Dwyer
articles.abstract:
Background: 
Language disturbances in psychotic and affective disorders persist beyond general cognitive dysfunction, yet the diagnostic value of computationally derived linguistic features relative to standard cognitive assessment remains unclear.
Aims: 
To evaluate whether features derived using natural language processing (NLP) methods from a brief verbal fluency task (VFT) allow classification of early-stage psychotic and affective disorders or if language-based classifiers demonstrate transdiagnostic generalizability.
Methods: 
Forty individuals with recent-onset psychosis (ROP), 50 with recent-onset depression (ROD), 33 at clinical high-risk for psychosis (CHR), and 54 healthy controls (HC) from the PRONIA study were investigated. Sixty-two NLP features were extracted from verbal fluency tasks. Five elastic net classifiers were evaluated using nested cross-validation, with cross-group transfer and staging analyses assessing severity across the four groups.
Results: 
NLP-only models achieved classification accuracy comparable to a four-domain cognitive battery (MCCB) across all comparisons (pFDR > 0.54). Classifiers trained on one diagnostic comparison generalized to others. Staging analysis confirmed the predicted dimensional ordering HC < ROD = CHR < ROP. Group differences were driven by verbal productivity and search organization rather than semantic coherence.
Conclusions: 
A two-minute verbal fluency assessment with automated NLP analysis matches a comprehensive cognitive battery for diagnostic classification. Linguistic features capture a differential-diagnostic marker that generalizes across diagnostic boundaries and reflects a dimensional severity gradient, supporting their integration into clinical screening or monitoring for early psychotic and affective disorders.

articles.title: “Now You Know, Don’t Be Indifferent”:  The Emotional Effects of Learning About Historical Violence and Their Relationship with Current Pro-Social Motivation
articles.authors: Máté Szilassi; Bertjan Doosje; Hanna Szekeres
articles.abstract: How does learning about historical violence inspire prosocial action today? Prior work has focused on whether such experiences are (emotionally) impactful but seldom tested the mechanisms of impact. We propose a framework of actor-specific emotional pathways connecting knowledge of historical violence to contemporary upstanding intentions: empathy for victims, moral emotions toward exemplars (e.g., admiration), and toward perpetrators (e.g., guilt). We tested this in a pre-registered study among Dutch youths (N = 1,371) participating in a Holocaust museum program, using Structural Equation Modeling to compare post-visit responses to a control group. We found that the program increased empathy for victims, positive emotions toward exemplars, and negative emotions toward perpetrators compared to the control group. However, only the “victim path” (empathy) mediated the program’s effect on upstanding intentions against antisemitic bullying. These findings highlight the central role of victim-centered narratives and inform educational efforts to foster present-day moral engagement.

articles.title: Unequal Selection? Assumably Criminogenic Personality Traits Cannot Explain the Gender Gap in Incarceration
articles.authors: Marie Joséphine Hamatschek; Jesko Niggemann
articles.abstract: The gender gap in incarceration is universal. Personality-based explanations imply that incarceration 'selects' on the same dispositions in both genders, with the same threshold. This equal selection model (ESM) yields three conditions for a trait to account for the gender gap: (a) a baseline gender difference, (b) an incarceration effect in both genders, and (c) threshold equivalence: incarcerated women resemble incarcerated men. We tested these predictions for six crime-associated traits, comparing incarcerated men (n = 115), incarcerated women (n = 39), and non-incarcerated men (n = 109) and women (n = 123). The ESM was not supported. Where a baseline gender difference and incarceration effects were confirmed (psychopathic meanness, Machiavellianism, guilt proneness), thresholds were inequivalent; where thresholds were equivalent (psychopathic disinhibition), no baseline gender difference was found. Descriptively, narcissistic entitlement came closest to the ESM pattern. Ultimately, even gender- and risk-associated personality traits might not account for the gender gap.

articles.title: Psychometric Properties of the Cardiac Distress Inventory: Classical and Modern Test Theory using Dynamic Fit Index Cutoffs
articles.authors: Philip Leissner; Erik Martin Gustaf Olsson; Niclas Almén; Ulf Elofsson; Michael Le Grande; Alun Jackson; Magnus Johansson
articles.abstract:
Background: Psychological distress is common in patients after a cardiac event. There is a need for validated questionnaires that capture the entire range of psychological reactions following such a condition. The Cardiac Distress Inventory (CDI) was developed in English and has already been translated into several languages, but there is no validated Swedish translation. Furthermore, the field of psychometric evaluation is constantly evolving, and the questionnaire has not yet been validated using the latest methods.
Methods: The CDI was translated into Swedish using translation-back translation. It was then administered online via REDCap to 353 participants recruited via social media. Psychometric methods used were Rasch analysis and confirmatory factor analysis (CFA), using dynamic fit indices, and test-retest correlations. Data were fitted to the partial credit model (PCM), investigating dimensionality, ordering of response categories, invariance, targeting and measurement uncertainty. An iterative revision of each subscale and the CDI short form (CDI-SF) was conducted based on issues identified in the analyses. Using the PCM, and CFA with dynamic fit indices, the CDI-SF was re-analyzed using data from the Australian development study.
Results: The main findings were that neither of the subscales in the CDI long form, nor the CDI-SF fit the Rasch model. These results were consistent for both the Swedish and English versions. All CDI subscales demonstrated problems with item misfit and local dependency. Every item demonstrated disordered thresholds to varying degrees. Issues with invariance were observed for several variables, most notably age for the subscale cognitive challenges. Some subscales had substantial floor-effects. All revised subscales except death concern demonstrated acceptable reliability. Test-retest correlations for these subscales were acceptable to high (r = 0.69-0.87).
Conclusions: Psychometric evaluation using Rasch analysis and CFA with dynamic fit indices identified several limitations of the CDI subscales, and the CDI-SF. Iterative revisions led to new subscales with acceptable reliability. While these revised scales may be a reliable option for the Swedish CDI, their clinical utility need to be established. Furthermore, the findings highlight the need for further testing and examination of the CDI across populations.

articles.title: Addressing bias-based bullying in schools: Behavior change methods for stigma reduction
articles.authors: Roy A. Willems; Sarah E. Stutterheim; Hanneke Felten; John B. Pryor
articles.abstract: Bias-based bullying describes bullying behavior in an intergroup context in which someone is bullied because of their membership of a particular group. This constitutes a significant health concern for children with socially devalued identities, characteristics, and attributes. Compared to interpersonal bullying, bias-based bullying is driven by processes of stigma and, therefore, strategies focusing on stigma reduction need to be incorporated in intervention development. Chapter 21 aims to support intervention developers, policymakers, school personnel, and other professionals by providing an overview of evidence-based behavior change methods, with examples of practical applications, for tackling bias-based bullying. The first part of the chapter explains bias-based bullying within the context of stigma theory. We utilize Pryor and Reeder's (2011) model of stigma as a framework to differentiate between public stigma, stigma-by-association, structural stigma, and self-stigma. A brief literature review illustrates how these stigma types apply to key actors involved in bias-based bullying in schools. We identify main determinants that facilitate or reduce bias-based bullying. Building on these determinants, the second part of the chapter presents an overview of relevant behavior change methods derived from stigma theory to address bias-based bullying. The chapter concludes with the recommendation that addressing bias-based bullying requires comprehensive stigma-focused interventions.

articles.title: Development and Psychometric Validation of a French Version of The Organisational Stressor Indicator for Sport Performers: A Multi-Study Investigation
articles.authors: Valentin DUVERT--CHENEBERT; aurélie campagne; Rachel Arnold; David Trouilloud
articles.abstract: In recent decades, organisational stressors have increasingly been examined in competitive athletes. The Organisational Stressor Indicator for Sport Performers (OSI-SP) is a widely used tool for measuring these stressors. However, its use and validation in different languages are limited. This two-study project aimed to develop a French version of the OSI-SP and evaluate its psychometric properties among two independent samples of adolescent-athletes (study 1) and high-level student-athletes (study 2). Study 1 included 322 adolescent-athletes recruited from intensive training centers. Results bring support for the 5-factor structure of the 23-item version of the scale, and partial support for the discriminant validity of the subscales. Findings also demonstrated satisfactory internal consistency, as well as convergent, concurrent and criterion validity. Study 2 included 431 high-level student athletes. Results confirmed the psychometric properties of the French OSI-SP in terms of internal consistency and factorial structure, convergent, discriminant, concurrent, and criterion validities. Moreover, the second study revealed the strict invariance of the French version of the OSI-SP over a one-month period. Together, these two studies demonstrated the psychometric properties of the French version of the OSI-SP, providing a valid scale for assessing organisational stressors among French-speaking athletes.

articles.title: Testing Biopsychism: Commentary on Block’s (2025) “Can Only Meat Machines Be Conscious?”
articles.authors: Hakwan Lau
articles.abstract: There are (at least) two possible readings of Block’s recent suggestion that consciousness may depend on subcomputational biological mechanisms [1]. First, there could be distinct mechanisms that support the same computations, yet only some biological ones give rise to subjective experiences. These mechanisms may differ functionally in ways that cannot be characterized in purely computational terms. Alternatively, there could be distinct mechanisms that make no functional (including computational) difference whatsoever, yet only some biological ones give rise to subjective experiences. I will argue that this second view—Anti-Functionalism—clashes with known principles of evolution. The first, weaker view—Anti-Computationalism—when properly understood, should motivate experimental efforts that are feasible but rarely attempted.

articles.title: Development and validation of a Japanese version of the Sensory Experience Questionnaire (SEQ) 3.0: Structural differences of sensory features across autistic children
articles.authors: Takeshi Atsumi; Haruka Ito; Akane Shinjo; Tomoe Fukutomi; Yasuo Terao; Masakazu Ide; Kanae Matsushima
articles.abstract: People with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) often have unique sensory experiences. For both research and clinical purposes, tools that measure sensory features in autism are valuable; however, a gold-standard tool does not yet exist. The Sensory Experience Questionnaire (SEQ) 3.0 is a well-established caregiver-/parent-report questionnaire for autistic children and other neurodivergent individuals. This study aimed to develop and psychometrically validate the Japanese version of the SEQ 3.0 (SEQ-J). After validation, we examined the differences in sensory response patterns among children with ASD, those with other neurodevelopmental conditions, and typically developing (TD) children to determine whether the SEQ assesses ASD-specific sensory features. We recruited 154 children with ASD (44 with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder [ADHD] and 66 with other conditions) and 156 TD children, aged 6 to 12 years. Confirmatory factor analyses revealed an acceptable model fit for four content factors (sensory hyper- and hyporesponsiveness, sensory interests, repetitive and seeking behaviors, and enhanced perception) and six method factors (five sensory modalities and social context) in both children with and without ASD. Receiver operator characteristic (ROC) analyses showed that SEQ factors, except for enhanced perception, demonstrated good classification performance between ASD and TD groups. However, ROC analyses comparing children with ASD to those with comorbid neurodevelopmental conditions did not show satisfactory performance. We performed representational similarity analyses (RSAs) on all ten SEQ factors, comparing both between- and within-group data. The RSAs revealed no difference in within-group similarities between the ASD group with no other developmental conditions and ASD + ADHD groups, but a significant difference in the overall configuration of scores between diagnostic groups compared to other group combinations. These findings support the psychometric validity of the SEQ-J for measuring sensory response patterns specific to children with ASD. Additionally, the structural configuration of SEQ subscores may be useful for investigating sensory features across diagnostic conditions, including ASD.

articles.title: From Silence to Whisper: Climate Change in U.S. News Media, 1984–2025
articles.authors: Sanguk Lee; Anthony Leiserowitz; Lara Briggs; Huong Ha; Matthew H. Goldberg
articles.abstract: News media shape public perceptions of issue importance, yet the position of climate change within the broader media landscape over time remains poorly understood. We conducted a large-scale content analysis of 6.4 million articles published by five major U.S. news outlets—The New York Times, The Washington Post, The Wall Street Journal, USA Today, and CNN—spanning 1984 to 2025. Using large language models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini), we classified each article across 16 news topics and identified climate change references. Over four decades, climate change comprised just 0.55% of all news coverage—one-thirty-sixth the attention devoted to sports and entertainment. Change-point detection identified two structural shifts that elevated baseline coverage: one in late 2006 and another in early 2021, coinciding with major political and cultural developments. Notably, once elevated, baseline coverage did not return to prior levels despite subsequent political shifts that introduced uncertainty around climate discourse. However, the integration of climate change across news domains remains highly uneven. Climate references are concentrated in environmental reporting while nearly absent from domains such as health and social justice—topics through which the public experiences climate impacts in daily life. Analysis of event-driven coverage revealed that international climate conferences (COP events) produced only transient spikes, while major hurricanes generated no detectable increase in climate reporting. These findings suggest that despite gradual progress, climate change has yet to achieve the cross-domain media presence commensurate with its significance as a societal challenge.

articles.title: What Drives Learning During Practice? Contributions of Exposure, Retrieval, and Error Correction
articles.authors: Meng Cao; Gillian Gold; Paulo F. Carvalho
articles.abstract: Practice can improve learning, yet the mechanisms through which learning changes across practice remain unclear. Existing theories propose that learning can arise through repeated exposure, error correction, and successful retrieval, but the relative contributions of these mechanisms are rarely evaluated systematically. In the present work, we use a computational modeling approach to examine how these mechanisms jointly contribute to learning. Across two learning contexts, results showed that the mechanisms supporting learning differed across practices with and without feedback. When feedback was provided, learning was best explained by a combination of exposure and effortful successful retrieval, whereas the contribution of error correction depended on the complexity of the learning materials and the type of feedback provided. When feedback was absent, learning primarily depended on retrieval success, with no additional contribution of retrieval effort. These findings suggest that learning is a context-dependent process in which the contributions of multiple mechanisms vary across task structures and feedback conditions. The present work provides a unified framework for understanding how these mechanisms contribute to learning over time.

articles.title: Why “Not Different” Is Not the Same as “Equivalent”: An Introduction to Equivalence Testing in Psychological Research
articles.authors: Sunthud Pornprasertmanit
articles.abstract: In psychological research, researchers may wish to examine whether two groups have similar means on a target variable, such as whether online and in-class learning are equally effective. However, researchers often rely on traditional null hypothesis testing (TNHST) to claim equivalence based on non-significant results, an approach that may lead to misleading conclusions. This article explains the limitations of this practice and introduces the concepts and methods of equivalence testing, also referred to as the negligible effect significance test. In brief, researchers specify a smallest effect size of interest (SESOI), below which an effect is considered practically negligible. They then test whether the observed statistic is significantly smaller than this threshold. If so, it can be concluded that the population effect is sufficiently small to be considered negligible. The article also provides examples of applications in psychological research and demonstrates how equivalence can be evaluated using outputs from commonly used statistical software or online tools.

articles.title: Vicious or Virtuous? Designing Generative AI-Powered Learning Experiences to Foster Rather than Undermine Critical Thinking in K-12 Education
articles.authors: Salah Esmaeiligoujar; Seyedahmad Rahimi
articles.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (GenAI) is rapidly entering K–12 classrooms, yet its effects on students’ critical thinking are still unclear. This systematic scoping review synthesizes empirical research published between 2017 and 2025 to examine how GenAI shapes critical thinking in K–12 education. From an initial pool of 1,392 studies, 38 met the inclusion criteria and were analyzed through inductive thematic coding. We categorized the included studies into three themes : (1) how GenAI supports critical thinking, (2) how GenAI undermines critical thinking, and (3) what are stakeholders’ perspectives on GenAI and critical thinking. The findings show that GenAI does not produce a single, uniform effect on learners’ reasoning. The same technology can strengthen or weaken critical thinking depending on how students interact with it. GenAI strengthens critical thinking when design requires learners to evaluate, question, and revise AI-generated content; GenAI weakens critical thinking when design allows students to accept outputs without evaluation and bypasses the inquiry, analysis, and metacognitive monitoring through which reasoning develops. Based on these findings, this review proposes a conceptual framework organized around two contrasting pathways: a virtuous cycle, in which cognitive scaffolding, dialogic interaction, adaptive feedback, and reflective design sustain active reasoning and build critical thinking over time, and a vicious cycle, in which cognitive substitution, over-directive interaction, misaligned feedback, and uncritical acceptance progressively erode it. The review concludes with implications for researchers, teachers, designers, and policymakers, and identifies directions for future empirical work in this rapidly evolving field.

articles.title: Chunking improves working memory via compression, not direct retrieval from long-term memory
articles.authors: Juan Manuel Chau Delgado; Joel Shebioba; Anna Pitt; Matias J. Ison; Nicholas E. Myers
articles.abstract: Working memory (WM) capacity is severely limited. Chunking, the process of grouping related items in WM, circumvents this limit and drastically improves performance. Despite decades of research, the mechanism underlying this benefit remains debated. One account proposes that statistical regularities allow multiple items to be compressed into structured WM representations, whereas another argues that structured associations are stored in long‑term memory (LTM) and reinstated at recall, effectively offloading storage demands. These accounts imply fundamentally different interactions between WM and long-term knowledge, yet in most paradigms, they make indistinguishable behavioural predictions. Here, we dissociate them by introducing small, continuous trial‑to‑trial variations in the hues of learned colour pairs, allowing us to test whether chunking benefits arise from compressed memory for the current hue (WM) or retrieval of long‑run feature means (LTM). Across two delayed colour report experiments, chunking reliably improved recall relative to unchunkable arrays. Participants remained sensitive to trial‑specific feature variations, indicating that responses were not driven by retrieval of feature means from LTM templates. At the same time, responses were systematically biased toward each item’s long‑run mean, especially on chunkable trials. To explain this bias, we fit psychophysical models derived from the competing compression and LTM‑retrieval accounts. The compression-based model, in which compression produces graded biases toward learned means within WM, outperformed the LTM-retrieval and baseline models. In a second experiment, we examined whether these biases arise during encoding/maintenance or at the recall stage. Cues in the maintenance period indicating which item would be probed improved recall accuracy but did not reduce bias toward learned structure, indicating that the bias arose during encoding or early maintenance rather than retrieval. These findings show that chunking enhances WM by integrating learned structure into WM representations through compression, rather than by offloading to LTM.

articles.title: Olfactory Disgust Conditioning across Multiple Response Systems: The Role of Temporal Contiguity
articles.authors: Anna Maria Schippers; Johannes Bernd Finke; Tim Klucken
articles.abstract: Disgust plays an important role in the development and maintenance of various mental disorders, with Pavlovian conditioning proposed as a potential underlying mechanism. Yet, psychophysiological correlates of disgust conditioning remain poorly understood, especially in olfactory conditioning, despite the notion that odors may be particularly effective in eliciting disgust. Disgust acquisition has also been theorized to rely comparatively less on the temporal contiguity between conditioned (CS) and unconditioned stimuli (UCS) (i.e., trace vs. delay conditioning), but there is little evidence to support this assumption. Based on a comprehensive assessment of self-report measures (valence, disgust, arousal, and expectancy ratings) and psychophysiological markers (respiratory latency [RSP], heart-period [HP] modulation, skin conductance response [SCR], and pupil dilation response [PDR]) in different response windows, the present study aimed to advance the understanding of disgust conditioning and to examine whether the degree of temporal contiguity of CS and UCS modulates conditioned responses. A total of 71 participants (54 women) underwent a within-subjects design consisting of two conditioning sessions with faces as CS and individualized aversive odors as UCS. Between sessions, the temporal contiguity of CS and UCS (delay conditioning: 2s overlap between CS and UCS; trace conditioning: 4s gap interval between CS offset and UCS onset) was varied. Evidence for differential conditioning (CS+ vs. CS-) was found across all self-report and most psychophysiological measures in both CS- and UCS-locked response windows. Trace conditioning was associated with attenuated responding but not reduced response differentiation in HP modulation, SCR, and PDR (both windows), with an interaction emerging only for RSP (CS-locked window). Controlling for trial-by-trial changes in RSP did not abolish previously identified conditioning effects, despite significant modulation of HP by RSP. Taken together, these findings demonstrate the applicability of autonomic measures established in fear conditioning research to disgust conditioning, with converging evidence across multiple response systems.

articles.title: The zebrafish alarm response as embodied semiosis: An approach between enactivism and biosemiotics
articles.authors: Thaeny Regina Ribeiro Rodrigues; Diego Frank Marques Cavalcante; Caio Maximino
articles.abstract: Mechanistic approaches in behavioral neuroscience often overlook the situated, embodied, and meaning‑generating dimensions of animal behavior. This paper builds a theoretical bridge between enactivism and biosemiotics – two frameworks that place sense‑making at the core of life – and grounds the synthesis in an empirical case: the alarm reaction of zebrafish (Danio rerio). The conspecific alarm substance (CAS) is not merely a chemical trigger but a semiotic sign that acquires meaning through the fish’s sensorimotor engagement with its ecological and social context. Using Peircean concepts, we analyze how the sign‑vehicle (the CAS mixture), its dynamic object (predation risk), and its interpretants (defensive actions, physiological changes) are enacted across multiple scales: from an individual’s sensorimotor loops to the emergent, collective interpretation of a shoal. This “embodied semiosis” perspective reveals that meaning is not pre‑given but constituted through the animal’s active, value‑laden interaction with its Umwelt. By integrating semiopsychology, which operationalizes semiotic categories for empirical inquiry, with enactivism’s emphasis on embodiment and agency, the framework yields testable predictions about how social coupling, context, and allostatic regulation shape alarm responses. We discuss operational challenges and the risk of overgeneralizing collective interpretants, while arguing that the approach offers a fertile alternative to representationalist accounts of adaptive behavior. Embodied semiosis thus provides a common language for studying how organisms, from zebrafish to humans, enact significance through their living, sense‑making bodies.

articles.title: Disinhibition and Anankastia as Distinct Domains of Maladaptive Control
articles.authors: Janan Mostajabi; Aidan G.C. Wright
articles.abstract: The nature of associations between disinhibition, anankastia, and conscientiousness as overlapping domains of control is not well understood. Using confirmatory bifactor analysis across two preregistered samples of undergraduates and community adults (total N = 1,068), this study parsed the shared and unique components of trait disinhibition and anankastia, and examined their associations with trait conscientiousness and relevant outcomes. Across both samples, disinhibition and anankastia were represented by specific latent factors, but a coherent general factor capturing shared variance across the two domains did not clearly emerge. The disinhibition-specific factor was modestly and negatively related to the anankastia-specific factor and strongly and negatively related to latent conscientiousness. In contrast, the anankastia-specific factor was moderately and positively related to latent conscientiousness, suggesting modest overlap with adaptive self-regulation. In terms of criterion validity, disinhibition showed the most consistent and robust associations with poorer functioning and behavioral addiction outcomes, whereas associations for anankastia were fewer and more modest. Together, these findings suggest that disinhibition and anankastia are largely distinct trait dimensions. Furthermore, disinhibition and conscientiousness appear to reflect closely related but opposing traits, whereas anankastia and conscientiousness are positively related but separable, with implications for personality pathology and over- and under-control tendencies.

articles.title: GenAI as Complete Cognitive Automation for Untrained Users at Population Scale: Implications for Human Factors and Ergonomics
articles.authors: Richard Pak; Ericka Rovira; Anne Collins McLaughlin
articles.abstract: Generative AI (GenAI) systems provide complete cognitive task outputs to untrained users across unrestricted domains at population scale, creating an unprecedented form of automation. We apply Parasuraman et al.’s (2000) framework of four cognitive automation stages to characterize this shift. GenAI automates all four stages simultaneously: information acquisition, information analysis, decision selection, and action specification. Classical automation effects (e.g., the “lumberjack effect”) were established exclusively in trained operators working within bounded domains. Whether these effects persist, intensify, or transform when GenAI violates all previous scope conditions simultaneously remains an open empirical question. Nearly a billion people now use this technology without the empirical foundation needed to predict human performance consequences. This commentary maps how GenAI’s deployment configuration exceeds automation theory’s empirical boundaries and identifies critical research questions to guide investigation of human performance and safety in this understudied area of automation design.

articles.title: Behavioral goal-setting for veterans with trauma distress: A qualitative report of goals and outcomes in a self-guided intervention
articles.authors: Joanna Fagan; Lindsay Lange; Adam P McGuire
articles.abstract: Goal setting is a key component to psychotherapy; however, little is known about self-generated goals of veterans with trauma distress. In this study, we explored goals set by veterans within a positive psychology intervention. Qualitative data were collected in an web-based intervention study that targeted positive social processes by eliciting moral elevation—feeling inspired by others’ virtuous actions. The sample included veterans who endorsed significant PTSD symptoms and moral injury distress. Participants completed 8 web-based, self-guided sessions that included setting their own goals and reporting on the outcomes in subsequent sessions using text responses. All qualitative data were coded using an inductive approach by study personnel. Separate raters reviewed all codes independently to identify themes. The most common themes of self-generated goals included prosocial behavior, enhancing family relationships, and self-improvement activities. Themes for successful outcome reactions included positive emotions and noticing a positive impact on others. Common themes for barriers included other responsibilities, interfering negative emotions, lack of motivation, and personal limitations. Strategies to overcome barriers were also identified. These qualitative findings offer a novel contribution by providing greater context for behavioral goal pursuits among veterans with trauma distress and warrant further examination into the goal setting process for this population.

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence for Precision Brain Stimulation: From Neuroimaging to Foundation Models and Virtual Brains
articles.authors: Meysam Hashemi; Wenqi Shi; Ghazaleh Soleimani; Ismael Villanueva-Miranda; Aref Pariz; Sheeba Anteraper; Jing Jin; Sina Mansour L.; Danilo Benette Marques; Mina Mirjalili
articles.abstract: Precision brain stimulation is an emerging paradigm in clinical neuroscience, offering the potential to modulate dysfunctional circuits with individualized protocols for disorders ranging from neurological to psychiatric disorders. The convergence of large-scale neuroimaging datasets, physics-based computational models, and artificial intelligence (AI) is rapidly accelerating this vision. This review integrates developments across four domains. First, we examine the availability and harmonisation of population-level neuroimaging repositories such as the UK Biobank, Human Connectome Project (HCP), Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study, and ENIGMA consortium, which provide unprecedented insight into structural and functional variability relevant to stimulation targeting biomarkers. Second, we trace the evolution of physics-based and computational head models that simulate electric, magnetic, and acoustic field distributions across non-invasive modalities—including transcranial electrical stimulation (tES), temporal interference stimulation (TIS), transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), and transcranial focused ultrasound (tFUS)—as well as invasive techniques such as cortical and deep brain stimulation. Third, we highlight the emergence of AI-driven brain imaging foundation models, such as NeuroSTORM, which leverage large-scale pre-training to enable predictive modeling of brain states, treatment response, and individualized stimulation optimization. Fourth, we present the concept of individualized digital twin brains—computational avatars that integrate neuroimaging, physics-based simulations, and AI foundation models to simulate and test stimulation strategies tailored to each patient’s connectivity and activation profile. Finally, we illustrate translational potential through two clinical use-case scenarios: precision targeting for seizure suppression in epilepsy and individualized stimulation for craving reduction and relapse prevention in addiction. By bridging databases, physics, and AI, this review outlines a roadmap for precision brain stimulation, emphasizing not only technological advances but also the clinical, ethical, and translational implications for real-world practice.

articles.title: Is the late positive potential (LPP) related to memory consolidation?
articles.authors: Hannah Piccirilli; Eric C Fields
articles.abstract: The late positive potential (LPP) is the component of the event-related potential (ERP) most consistently modulated by the emotional properties of stimuli. This component has been investigated in many studies, but little is known about the cognitive mechanism it reflects. In this study, we tested a model suggesting that the LPP reflects processes related to memory encoding and consolidation. To investigate this, we recorded EEG while participants read a series of neutral and negative words. Memory was then tested at both short (10-15 minutes) and long (12+ hours, including a period sleep) delays. We predicted that LPP amplitude would be positively correlated with memory, and that this relationship would be stronger after a delay (reflecting a relationship between LPP amplitude and memory consolidation). Consistent with our predictions, we found that LPP amplitude predicted memory in the delayed memory test, but not the immediate memory test. In addition, memory for negative items, compared to neutral items, declined less across the delay, and LPP amplitude mediated this effect of emotion on memory decay.

articles.title: Preschoolers can coordinate with each other to communicate about novel referents
articles.authors: Veronica Boyce; Robert Zane Sparks; Michael C. Frank
articles.abstract: Learning language requires learning not only the content of language, but also how to use language to communicate. Iterated reference games provide a window into such skills, requiring rich communication as participants converge on mutually understandable names for initially novel referents. Some early experiments are interpreted as evidence that 4-5-year-old children cannot converge on the mutually understandable names needed to succeed in an iterated reference game. Here, we revisit young children's referential communicative abilities using a simpler, child-friendly version of the iterated reference game paradigm. Across 3 experiments and 82 pairs of children (N=158), we found that 4-5-year-olds could successfully establish reference with each other. Children were 81% accurate, and they often used descriptions similar to their partner's. These findings suggest that children’s capacity to construct effective referring expressions in novel contexts emerges earlier than previously thought, consistent with the view that children show early pragmatic competence in supportive contexts.

articles.title: Characterizing the frequency and content of speech from other children in daylong recordings of infants’ input
articles.authors: Federica Bulgarelli; Marzie Samimifar; Giselle Yinjie Yao
articles.abstract: Infant’s language development is inherently tied to their linguistic input, but the majority of research investigating infant’s input has focused on adult caregiver speech.  However, children do not exclusively interact with adults. In the US, 80% of children’s households include additional children. The focus on adult caregiver input thus does not fully capture the experience of the majority of children.  We quantify infant’s experiences with speech from children, measuring its frequency and content. We find that speech from children is a common feature of the input for infants with older siblings, occurring once every minute. Analyses of content find that children produce the same words more often (lower type-token ratio) and shorter utterances overall, and that their speech contains earlier learned words and more nouns. Speech from children however does not vary from speech from adults in average word frequency or in iconicity. We highlight the importance of considering speech from children for infants with older siblings in our theories of language development.

articles.title: Representational Idiosyncrasy of Psychopathology Across Text Embedding Models: Implications and Practical Suggestions for Clinical Psychological Science
articles.authors: Jared T Gabrielli; Whitney R. Ringwald; Camilo Ruggero; Leonard Simms; Roman Kotov; Nicholas R Eaton
articles.abstract: The integration of large language models (LLMs) into clinical psychological science has outpaced our understanding of how psychopathology is represented across the diverse model landscape. We analyzed how 21 text embedding models represented 405 items from the HiTOP-SR, a non-public psychopathology measure absent from training data. Exploratory factor analysis of item-level representations at global and domain-specific levels revealed variable structural consistency across models. Globally, structural congruence frequently fell below meaningful thresholds (CC < .85), although broad two-factor solutions showed strong cross-model convergence. Domain-specific congruence varied considerably, with some domains (e.g., Anankastia) showing strong convergence and others (e.g., Antagonism) showing weak convergence. These findings demonstrate that text embedding models may lack a shared representational framework for psychopathology, yielding structures that are idiosyncratic and sensitive to model-specific features and methodological choices. These inconsistencies underscore the need for careful model selection and rigorous evaluation of LLM-based tools.

articles.title: Aquahenosis: A non-pharmacological altered state of consciousness induced by Floatation-REST in individuals with anxiety and depression
articles.authors: Theo Tobel; Aidan Cone; Emily Choquette; McKenna Garland; Micah Johnson; Keller Mink; Caitlin Lynch; Joel Frohlich; Justin Feinstein; Nicco Reggente
articles.abstract: Floatation-REST (Reduced Environmental Stimulation Therapy) systematically alters sensory and bodily input by combining neutral buoyancy, thermal and proprioceptive neutrality, attenuation of exteroceptive stimulation, and enhancement of cardiorespiratory signaling to the brain. Here we examined whether this non-pharmacological sensory perturbation induces altered states of consciousness and whether specific experiential dimensions are statistically related to changes in affect. In a secondary analysis of a randomized controlled feasibility trial, 75 treatment-seeking adults with anxiety and depression were assigned to six sessions of Floatation-REST with prescribed scheduling, Floatation-REST with preferred scheduling and duration, or a zero-gravity chair comparison condition. Altered states of consciousness were assessed using the 5-Dimensional Altered States of Consciousness Rating Scale, alongside measures of interoceptive awareness and affect. Compared to the chair condition, Floatation-REST was associated with increased interoceptive awareness of cardiorespiratory sensations and an altered state of consciousness characterized by Oceanic Boundlessness, Disembodiment, and Experience of Unity—a pattern we refer to as "aquahenosis." Effects were strongest among participants who selected longer and more flexible float sessions. Experiential profiles selectively overlapped with those reported for psilocybin and ketamine along boundary-dissolution dimensions. These findings identify Floatation-REST as a tractable, non-pharmacological method for inducing specific altered states of consciousness and highlight oceanic boundlessness as an important mediator of the float-induced changes in positive affect.

articles.title: Language-Based Detection of Adherence to Evidence-Based Psychotherapy Scripts
articles.authors: Samuel Thomas Campione; Elizabeth Stade; Stefanie T. LoSavio; Shreya Singhvi; William Xuan; Phi Long Bui; Maria Martin Lopez; Shashanka Subrahmanya; Bailee B. Schuhmann; Courtney B. Worley
articles.abstract: Some psychotherapies, such as written exposure therapy for posttraumatic stress disorder, utilize “scripts” during parts of treatment, but verifying script adherence to ensure engagement of key mechanisms of change is a time consuming step for therapy supervisors. Here, we formalize therapy script adherence as an NLP task, and evaluate several simple (text similarity) and more complex (few-shot LLM) approaches. Over 351 annotated therapist utterance-script pairs, we find text similarity approaches to be highly competitive with LLMs and produce fewer false positives. ROUGE-L recall achieves F1 = 0.973, and BLEU achieves F1 = 0.972 with full precision and zero false positives. GPT-5.2 achieves F1 = 0.935 and GPT-4o-mini achieves F1 = 0.876. Given that the text similarity techniques are multiple orders of magnitude less complex, our results underscore the ability of simpler NLP techniques to still be effective in the age of LLMs for tasks that are more textual in nature and suggest their utility in clinical training for giving supervisees rapid, privacy-preserving feedback on their delivery of evidence-based psychotherapy scripts.

articles.title: Social Influence in Donation Behavior: The Effect of Mean, Variance, and Psychopathy
articles.authors: Tao Jin; Shen Zhang; Xinyuan Yan; Iris Vilares
articles.abstract: Charitable giving is a cornerstone of social welfare, and others’ donations can promote individual giving. However, the computational mechanisms through which social information shapes donation behavior, and how individuals differ in their responsiveness, remain poorly understood. Here, we investigated how the statistical properties of others’ donations shape individual giving, and how this was affected by individual differences in socio-affective traits. Across 4 pre-registered experiments (total N = 1,491), participants made donations before and after observing sequential donations from diverse others whose mean and variability were systematically manipulated. We found that observing generous average donations robustly increased individual giving, while observing stingy donations decreased it. Moreover, the variance in social information did not modulate this shift. In parallel, exposure to others’ donations consistently reduced the variability of individual donations, with stronger reductions when others’ donations were more consistent (versus more variable). Computational modeling revealed that a hybrid reinforcement learning model integrating initial donations with learned predictions of others’ donations captured participants’ donation behaviors well. At the individual level, changes in donations following social exposure were consistently positively associated with psychopathic traits. Model-derived parameters further revealed that people with higher psychopathic traits were more susceptible to social information, assigning greater weight to observed donations when making their second decisions. These results were replicated across all experiments, despite variations in donation magnitudes, population type, variability discrepancy, and incentive structures. Together, our findings demonstrate that the statistical structure of social information - its magnitude and variability - shapes the corresponding features of individual donations, and identify psychopathy as a key factor underlying heterogeneity in social influence.

articles.title: Beyond Right and Wrong: Evaluating Second-order Social Reasoning in Large Language Models
articles.authors: Sunny Rai; Jinyi Kuang; Reyhan Jamalova; Annie Lou; Cristina Bicchieri; Niyati Malhotra; Victor Hugo Orozco Olvera; Ana Maria Munoz-Boudet; Lyle H Ungar; Sharath C. Guntuku
articles.abstract: Previous AI alignment efforts have focused primarily on first-order social norms -- teaching models what is socially acceptable or unacceptable (e.g., "do not steal"). However, social intelligence depends not only on norm recognition, but also on anticipating who will enforce it and how (e.g., public shame or even imprisonment).  These second-order expectations, known as metanorms, govern how people respond when social rules are broken.  We introduce a novel framework for evaluating metanorm reasoning in Large Language Models (LLMs) along two dimensions: emotional appraisal and behavioral response, and propose new classification tasks, namely, predicting self-regulation in violators, and other-regulation in observers. We release a multi-perspective dataset, NormReact, of 450 norm violation scenarios, hand-annotated for emotions and behavioral responses across norm violators' gender and observers' social closeness. Current LLMs portray a harsher social world: across six models, they overpredict negative sanctions where humans would expect inaction, and alignment with human judgments deteriorates as social distance increases. These findings suggest that AI systems in norm-sensitive domains, from conflict mediation to policy simulation, may risk producing a distorted picture of social regulation: one that over-represents punishment and under-represents the tolerance, restraint, and relational calibration that characterize actual norm enforcement in the real world.

articles.title: Can we Automate Social Science Concept Harmonization? A Fine-Grained Review of Performance and Failure Modes
articles.authors: Rasoul Norouzi; Bennett Kleinberg; Jeroen Vermunt; Caspar J. Van Lissa
articles.abstract: Conceptual ambiguity is a persistent obstacle to cumulative social science: the same construct appears under different labels, while a single label can stand for distinct constructs. Text-embedding methods offer a scalable route to harmonization, but their value depends on recovering conceptual overlap rather than surface-level lexical similarity. We evaluate 12 pipelines that combine four embedding models with three techniques, calibrated on the European Language Social Science Thesaurus and tested on the APA Thesaurus. Beyond accuracy, five diagnostic tests examine lexical traps, semantic decay, stability, rare-word bias, and alignment with expert taxonomic structure. Harmonization identifies many synonymous labels, but accuracy is uneven and depends on surface form, with errors concentrated in cases central to theory development: low-overlap synonyms, rare terms, and subtle taxonomic distinctions. The diagnostic tests make these successes and failures transparent, giving researchers a reproducible basis for deciding when to trust embedding-based harmonization and when to defer to experts.

articles.title: Between the Steeple and the Golden Stool: Navigating Cultural Conflict, Pentecostal Competition, and the Organisational Resilience Paradox in Asante Baptist Ministry
articles.authors: McFaustine Kofi Mahama; Yaw Adu-Gyamfi; Gabriel Osei Forkuo
articles.abstract:
Background: The intersection of Asante cosmological traditions and Christian ministry constitutes a profoundly contested space in West African Christianity. Despite severe implications for denominational attrition amid aggressive Pentecostal competition, empirical examination of this culture-faith nexus remains sparse. This pioneering study investigates the prevalence, character, and congregational consequences of conflicts between traditional Asante practices and Baptist theology in urban Ghana.
Methods: Employing a robust cross-sectional mixed-methods design, we surveyed 200 ordained Ghana Baptist Convention pastors in the Kumasi Metropolis. Quantitative data underwent rigorous statistical evaluation using Python 3.12, including chi-square tests of independence and Spearman’s rank-order correlation, triangulated with keyword-based thematic analysis of qualitative pastoral narratives.
Results: Findings reveal that 70.5% of pastors consistently navigate culture-faith conflicts, predominantly driven by deeply entrenched taboos (18.0%), bride-price demands (17.0%), and ancestral worship (15.0%). Notably, while 23.0% of pastors reported direct membership attrition to culturally accommodating Pentecostal alternatives, chi-square analysis established no significant statistical association between conflict frequency and attrition (χ² = 6.888, p = 0.549). Spearman correlations demonstrated that pastoral challenge domains operate independently of one another (|ρ|≤0.133). Crucially, the data expose a novel "organisational resilience paradox": despite experiencing profound cultural friction, intense Pentecostal competition, and compounding youth apathy (68.0%), 88.5% of pastors report high congregational effectiveness in community impact.
Conclusions: This study provides pioneering empirical evidence of the structural friction between the "steeple and the stool" in modern Ghana. To sustain institutional vitality against Pentecostal migration, historic mission churches must pivot from rigid prohibition toward a systematic, Asante-informed practical theology of inculturation without syncretism.

articles.title: Styles That Heal or Harm: Rethinking Leadership Through the Lens of Employee Well-Being
articles.authors: Cheryl Jolly; Lakshmi Verma
articles.abstract:
Introduction: Employee well-being (EWB) is a construct having multiple dimensions that consist of physical, psychological, environmental, and social dimensions. It has been observed that the leadership style of the managers or supervisors can significantly influence employee well-being by affecting the work culture, motivation, and overall emotional climate of the employees within the organisation. This narrative review aims to investigate how various leadership styles, specifically Transformational, Authentic, Servant, and Task-oriented leadership, impact the well-being of employees through mediating and moderating variables.
Method: Narrative review has been utilised to parse the relevant literature comprehensively, comprising both empirical and theoretical studies from PsycINFO, PubMed, and Google Scholar. The keyword used to find the studies includes “leadership style,” “transformational,” “authentic,” “servant,” and “employee well-being.” For review purposes, 20 peer-reviewed studies were selected that met the set criteria for inclusion while opinion papers and non-empirical works were excluded. Both cross-sectional and longitudinal studies were analysed to ensure comprehensive coverage.
Results: From the review, it has been found that transformational and authentic leadership show positive associations with employee well-being by means of various mediator variables like meaningful work, trust, and empowerment. On the other hand, servant leadership enhances psychological well-being and job satisfaction by keeping in priority the employees’ growth. Task-oriented leadership supports eudaimonic well-being by focusing on the structure and purpose of work. It has also been noted that various moderator variables, such as cultural context, psychological safety, and job stressors significantly shape the association between the variables.
Implications: From the review findings, the priority needs to be placed on developing leadership programs that emphasise authenticity, empathy, and ethical decision-making to increase the well-being of the employees and retain them for the long term within the organisation.

articles.title: How Ozempic is Changing Dating:  GLP-1 Weight Loss Medications Are Reshaping Body Confidence, Desire, and Stigma
articles.authors: Rhonda Nicole Balzarini; Justin J Lehmiller; Amanda N. Gesselman; Merissa Prine; Emily Mendelson; Selena Piercy
articles.abstract:
Background
Recent increases in the use of glucagon-like peptide-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1s) have generated widespread public interest in their physical and psychological effects, yet little research has examined how these medications may shape romantic and sexual experiences.

Aim
The present study investigated associations between GLP-1 use for weight loss and dating outcomes, sexual experiences, and perceived stigma among single adults in the United States.

Methods
Participants were 2,002 demographically representative single adults residing in the United States who completed measures assessing GLP-1 use, perceived changes in dating and sexual experiences, and concerns about judgment related to medication use.

Outcomes
Primary outcomes included perceived changes in dating experiences, sexual experiences, confidence, sexual functioning, and stigma associated with GLP-1 use.

Results
Overall, 7.7% of participants reported using GLP-1 medications for weight loss in the past year. Among users, approximately one-third reported improvements in their dating lives and sex lives, although substantial variability emerged across outcomes. Some participants reported increased confidence, greater dating engagement, improved sexual functioning, and enhanced body comfort, whereas others reported reduced dating interest, lower confidence, or worsened sexual experiences. Gender differences also emerged. Men were more likely than women to report increases in dating confidence, changes in dating behavior, improvements in sexual functioning, and greater concern about being judged for using GLP-1 medications. Women were more likely to report no changes in sexual outcomes and lower concern about stigma.

Clinical Translation
Healthcare and mental health professionals should recognize that GLP-1 medications may meaningfully shape patients’ romantic confidence, sexual experiences, and stigma concerns, highlighting the importance of addressing psychosocial and relational changes, not just physical outcomes, when initiating and managing GLP-1 treatment.

Strengths and Limitations
Strengths of the study include the use of a large, demographically representative sample and the examination of an emerging but understudied topic related to sexuality and relationships. Limitations include cross-sectional design, reliance on self-reported outcomes, and inability to determine causal associations between GLP-1 use and relational or sexual experiences.

Conclusion
The findings suggest that the relational and sexual consequences of GLP-1 use are heterogeneous and embedded within broader gendered, relational, and sociocultural contexts.

articles.title: Cooperation increases verbal and spatial coordination in human foraging groups in the wild
articles.authors: Marwa M Kavelaars; Alexander Schakowski; Dominik Deffner; Fleur Francien Adrienne Korzilius; Félicie Dhellemmes; Jens Krause; Raine Kortet; Ralf Kurvers
articles.abstract: The diversity of foraging styles across environments and social systems is a key driver of the success of our species. Yet the human foraging literature is largely observational, since experimental manipulations in situ are difficult. We investigated highly experienced foragers (ice fishers) in situ through direct experimental manipulations: Foragers fished alone or in groups, and participants in groups were incentivised to either compete or cooperate. High-precision GPS tracking and head-camera data recording foraging behaviour, catch success, and verbal communication, combined with cognitive-computational modelling, showed that foragers adaptively integrated socioecological information. Across all conditions, foragers travelled further when unsuccessful; in groups, they also considered the locations of group members when deciding where to go. Cooperative foragers communicated more, and approached others more frequently, but did not outperform competitive foragers. Our study highlights the adaptive nature of human foraging behaviour, demonstrating how group setting and incentive structures shape spatial decision-making and communication strategies.

articles.title: Social information use across the lifespan
articles.authors: Lucas Molleman; Zhan Feng; Wouter van den Bos; Deyan Dzhurov; Andrea Gradassi; Mehdi Moussaid; Wataru Toyokawa; Alan Novaes Tump; Katsumi Watanabe; Ralf Kurvers
articles.abstract: Social information shapes how humans learn, decide, and coordinate. Theory predicts that social information is most valuable early in life, but how social information use changes across the lifespan remains poorly understood. Across seven belief-updating experiments with museum samples from Germany and Japan (ages 6–99 years; N = 41,015), we find a robust age-related trajectory: Social information use is highest early in life, drops sharply until late adolescence, and then rises slowly into late adulthood. Participants at all ages used social information most when it was close to their own belief, supported by convergent social inputs, and came from young adults. Female participants used slightly more social information than male participants, and participants from Germany used more social information than those from Japan. Together, these results reveal early-emerging strategic social information use and help explain information flow in age-diverse populations.

articles.title: A phylogenetically conserved microstructural primitive of goal-directed action in humans and macaques
articles.authors: xiaosheng chen; xiaosheng chen
articles.abstract: The capacity to override well-learned automaticity in the service of explicit goals is foundational to flexible cognition, yet whether the dual-process architecture supporting context-dependent goal-directedness is a deep cognitive primitive or a contingent product of human culture, language, and instruction has remained untested. Recent work in humans suggests that operant responding is not a uniform stream but unfolds in short bursts of rapid actions separated by longer pauses, and that these bursts decompose into two behaviourally and computationally distinct components: within-burst responses, the rapid actions that sustain an already-ongoing burst (for example, the second, third, and subsequent presses in a quick run of presses), which carry an outcome-sensitive, goal-directed signature; and burst-initiation responses, the first action that breaks a pause and starts a new burst, which operates closer to a motivationally cached, model-free regime. The long-standing random-ratio versus random-interval (RR vs RI) difference in goal-directedness reflects a schedule-driven shift in the composition of these two components rather than a difference at the schedule level. Here we provide the first phylogenetic test of this microstructural primitive by directly comparing rhesus macaques (n = 3) and human adults (n = 60) on matched RR and yoked-RI schedules. IRT distributions in both species were well described by a common bi-exponential mixture, and Welch two one-sided tests against an a-priori ±1 SD(humans) bound established statistical equivalence on all three mixture parameters (λ_fast, λ_slow, p) under both schedules. Both species exhibited the same RR-driven elevation of the within-burst proportion, and the cross-species microstructural match was present from the earliest assessable macaque training stage and remained stable across mid- and late-training blocks. Under extinction and specific-satiety devaluation, within-burst responding fell first and farthest in both species, replicating its goal-directed signature, while burst-initiation was comparatively preserved. These results identify the bi-exponential dual-process microstructure of operant behaviour as a phylogenetically conserved cognitive primitive of action selection that predates the cultural and linguistic scaffolds of human action control and supply a directly translational measure for animal-model and clinical research on disorders of habitual and goal-directed control.

articles.title: Limes Master Edition: Using Behavioural Data to Refine Game Mechanics in Research
articles.authors: Amedeo Viccari
articles.abstract: Educational video games are increasingly used in experimental research to examine their effects on learning and motivation. However, many studies focus primarily on outcome measures such as pre–post knowledge tests or self-reported engagement, while paying limited attention to behavioural data generated during gameplay. As a result, it often remains unclear which specific game features contribute to learning outcomes. This paper argues that behavioural logging within educational games can play an important methodological role in experimental research by revealing how learners interact with key game mechanics and by informing the iterative refinement of these mechanics. I illustrate this argument using Limes, a Unity-based educational video game designed to teach Roman history, as a methodological case study based on previously conducted research. The paper draws on findings from two previously conducted experimental studies investigating different aspects of the game design. In these studies, behavioural logs captured players’ interactions with several gameplay features, including optional Codex entries that provided additional historical information. Analyses indicated that engagement with Codex content was positively associated with knowledge acquisition, highlighting the Codex system as a learning-relevant feature within the game environment. Based on these insights, the Codex system was iteratively redesigned during the continued development of the game, leading to a revised version (Limes Master Edition) with more precise behavioural tracking. While earlier versions tracked Codex engagement at a general level, the revised version implemented a logging architecture capable of recording interactions with individual Codex entries. This modification enables future research to analyse detailed patterns of information-seeking behaviour and to better understand how optional learning resources are used during gameplay. Using Limes as a case study, this paper discusses challenges that arise when educational games are used as experimental research tools. It illustrates how behavioural evidence from experiments can guide the refinement and instrumentation of game features. Building on this example, the paper proposes guidelines for researchers who develop educational games for experimental studies, outlining how behavioural data can inform the iterative development of game mechanics and logging systems. By linking experimental findings with design refinements, this approach supports a systematic investigation of how specific game features contribute to learning outcomes.

articles.title: Audio features impact the semantic content of music-evoked thoughts
articles.authors: Wei Wu; Tuomas Eerola; Elizabeth Hellmuth Margulis; Kelly Jakubowski
articles.abstract: Music permeates our everyday environments and can elicit a wide range of thoughts for the listener. To understand the mechanisms through which music steers ongoing thoughts, previous studies have investigated the effects of different music genres or excerpts on the content of music-evoked thoughts. However, 'genre' is a very broad and potentially ambiguous category (e.g., “pop music”) and 'excerpt' is too narrow to allow generalization of results. The current study explored how music shapes thoughts with a mid-level approach based on features derived from the audio. We collected open-text descriptions of music-evoked thoughts from approximately 1800 participants and extracted 27 audio features across seven acoustic domains from our music stimuli (120 instrumental excerpts from a range of genres). By applying natural language processing methods to the open-text data, we measured the general semantic similarity for the thoughts (via word2vec) and decomposed thought contents into 16 semantic topics (via Latent Dirichlet Allocation). Then we used regression models to map the audio profile of the music to the semantic profile of the thoughts. We found that the audio features effectively explained both variance in the general structure of the semantic similarity space and in the topic-specific contents of the thoughts. Our results demonstrate that auditory features serve as a window into how music impacts ongoing thoughts, providing a key method for future targeted investigations of how music can be used in everyday and clinical settings to shape listeners’ mental experience.

articles.title: Relationship between personal responsibility to reduce climate change and trust in politics: a brief multi-level analysis using data from 28 countries
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Gabija Jarašiūnaitė-Fedosejeva
